text:
Degree project 
Evaluating Retrieval Strategies for 
Domain-Specific RAG Systems 
through Automated Metrics and 
Human-Centered User Study 
 
 
 
 
Master Degree Project in Informatics with a 
specialization in Data Science  
Second Cycle 15 credits 
Autumn term 2025 
Student: Nihal Kayamkulam Abdulkhader 
Supervisor: Joe Steinhauer 
Examiner: Juhee Bae

Abstract 
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a promising paradigm for enhancing 
the factuality and contextual relevance of large language model (LLM) outputs, particularly in 
enterprise applications such as help-desk automation. This thesis evaluates three retrieval 
strategies—sparse (BM25), dense (SBERT + FAISS), and hybrid (Reciprocal Rank Fusion)—
combined with two open-source LLMs, Mistral-7B-Instruct and LLaMA-2-7B-Chat, to assess 
their performance in a domain-specific customer support scenario. Using the Doc2Dial 
dataset, we implement a RAG pipeline and assess model outputs using both automated metrics 
(Hit@K, nDCG@K, ROUGE-L, BERTScore, Cosine Similarity) and human evaluations across 
multiple dimensions (factuality, relevance, clarity, and completeness). Results show that dense 
and hybrid retrievers outperform sparse retrieval in both retrieval and generation quality, and 
Mistral-7B exhibits slightly stronger performance in human-rated clarity and completeness. A 
Pearson correlation analysis reveals that ROUGE-L aligns more closely with human judgment 
compared to other automated metrics. The thesis highlights the limitations of relying solely on 
automatic metrics and underscores the importance of qualitative human evaluation in 
enterprise-oriented RAG systems. Future work can explore more diverse domains, retrieval 
architectures, and fine-tuning strategies to further improve response quality and contextual 
grounding. 
Keywords: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG); Sparse Retrieval (BM25); Dense 
Retrieval (SBERT, FAISS); Hybrid Retrieval (Reciprocal Rank Fusion); Large Language 
Models (LLaMA-2-7B, Mistral-7B); Evaluation Metrics (Hit@K, MRR, nDCG, ROUGE-L, 
BERTScore, Cosine Similarity); Human Evaluation; Doc2Dial; Enterprise Help-Desk; 
Conversational AI.

1 
Introduction  
In recent years, advances in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and the development of Large 
Language Models (LLMs) have enabled the creation of intelligent conversational agents 
capable of generating fluent, human-like responses. However, despite these impressive 
capabilities, LLMs often struggle with factual consistency and contextual relevance, especially 
when operating in specialized domains such as technical customer support. Retrieval-
Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a promising paradigm that combines generative 
modelling with information retrieval to mitigate these issues (Lewis et al., 2020). 
As enterprises increasingly adopt AI-driven chatbots to automate and scale their customer 
support operations, the importance of delivering factually correct and contextually grounded 
responses cannot be overstated. A poor or incorrect response from a chatbot can reduce user 
trust, increase follow-up interactions, and ultimately lead to customer dissatisfaction. This is 
particularly problematic in high-stakes settings such as product troubleshooting, where users 
depend on accurate and specific information. 
This thesis explores how the choice of retrieval strategy in a RAG-based architecture, 
specifically sparse, dense, or hybrid methods, impacts the quality of AI-generated responses. 
By evaluating these retrieval methods using both human judgment and automatic metrics, this 
study aims to offer guidance to researchers and practitioners building domain-specific 
conversational systems. 
1.1 
Problem  
While the generative capabilities of large language models (LLMs) have grown substantially, a 
critical weakness remains: their tendency to hallucinate — generating text that is fluent but 
factually incorrect (Ji et al., 2023). This poses a significant risk in real-world applications, 
particularly in enterprise and technical support contexts, where factual accuracy is essential 
and misinformation can lead to costly errors. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) 
addresses this limitation by incorporating a retrieval module that supplies relevant context to 
the language model, grounding its responses in external sources (Lewis et al., 2020). 
However, not all retrieval strategies are equally effective. Sparse retrieval methods such as 
BM25 rely on keyword overlap and have long been considered a strong baseline for document 
ranking in information retrieval (Robertson & Zaragoza, 2009). Dense retrieval approaches 
instead leverage deep learning-based embeddings to capture semantic similarity, often 
outperforming sparse methods when exact keyword matching is insufficient (Karpukhin et al., 
2020). Libraries such as FAISS enable scalable dense retrieval across billions of vectors 
(Johnson et al., 2017). Hybrid methods, including Reciprocal Rank Fusion, combine sparse 
and dense retrieval to benefit from both lexical precision and semantic generalization 
(Cormack et al., 2009). 
In enterprise help-desk environments, users often interact with technical documentation or 
support systems via manual keyword search, static FAQs, or basic scripted chatbots. These 
approaches struggle to deliver relevant and timely answers, particularly as documentation 
grows in volume and complexity. Delayed responses, fragmented knowledge bases, and 
difficulty adapting to user-specific queries are common challenges (as further discussed in 
Section 2.1.1). In such settings, the choice of retrieval strategy in a RAG system has direct

2 
 
implications for support quality, efficiency, and user trust. 
While prior research has made progress in optimizing retrieval components, most studies 
focus primarily on retrieval accuracy and neglect the downstream impact on the generated 
output. Few have evaluated how different retrievers affect the clarity, factual correctness, and 
overall usefulness of RAG responses in real-world domains (see Section 2.2.5). Moreover, 
evaluations are often limited to automated metrics that may not reflect human judgment. 
This thesis addresses this problem by systematically comparing sparse, dense, and hybrid 
retrieval strategies within a domain-specific RAG system, using the Doc2Dial dataset as a 
benchmark. Beyond automated evaluation, the study incorporates a structured human 
evaluation with domain experts and end-users, offering insights into how retrieval strategies 
influence response quality in realistic enterprise scenarios. 
1.2 
Case Study  
This research is grounded in a real-world case inspired by the customer support department 
of a mining machinery manufacturing company. The department initiated exploratory work to 
integrate AI-powered chatbots into its support pipeline, aiming to reduce manual workload 
and enhance the speed and quality of end-user assistance. 
To ensure the system meets user expectations, semi-structured interviews were conducted 
with three customer support specialists. The participants consistently emphasized several 
critical qualities for AI-generated responses: factual accuracy, contextual grounding, 
avoidance of hallucinations, ability to handle follow-up questions, clarity, and professional 
tone. Interestingly, all participants agreed that stylistic alignment with the company’s 
communication style was less important than clarity and correctness. What mattered most was 
that responses should be factually reliable, precise, and transparent about their sources. 
This feedback provided valuable input into the evaluation framework of the thesis. Specifically, 
it guided the choice of human evaluation metrics, which include factual correctness, relevance, 
clarity and completeness. By aligning the study with real practitioner expectations, the 
research ensures both academic rigor and industrial relevance. 
To enable reproducibility and to simulate the type of interactions typical in customer support 
environments, this study uses a publicly available dialogue dataset that contains multi-turn 
conversations grounded in domain-specific documents. While the dataset does not originate 
from the same organization, its structure and goals closely resemble those of technical support 
systems, making it a suitable proxy for this research.  
1.3 
Aim and Research Question 
The overarching aim of this thesis is to investigate the relationship between retrieval strategy 
and the effectiveness of AI-generated responses in a RAG-based chatbot designed for domain-
specific support. 
 
Research Question: 
 
How do sparse, dense, and hybrid retrieval strategies affect the quality of responses 
generated by Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems for customer support tasks?

3 
 
This research question is both timely and practically relevant, given the current push in 
industry toward grounded conversational AI systems that minimize hallucination while 
maximizing user trust. 
1.3.1 Objectives 
To address the research question, the following objectives are defined: 
1. 
Design and implement six RAG pipelines combining three retrieval types (sparse with 
BM25, dense with FAISS, and hybrid) and two LLMs (Mistral 7B Instruct and LLaMA 2 
7B) (Touvron et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2023). 
2. 
Collect domain-specific expectations through interviews with customer support specialists 
to define human-centric evaluation criteria. 
3. 
Utilize a publicly available dialogue dataset that structurally resembles customer support 
interactions to simulate realistic query–response behaviour in the implementation and 
enable reproducibility. 
4. 
Evaluate system performance using human assessments on four dimensions: factual 
correctness, relevance, clarity, and completeness. Complement this with automated 
evaluation using standard text generation metrics (e.g., ROUGE-L, BERTScore) and 
retrieval performance metrics (e.g., Hit@k, MRR, nDCG) to assess the effectiveness of 
each RAG setup. 
5. 
Analyse and compare results to determine which retrieval approach performs best for 
customer support scenarios. 
1.4 
Contribution 
This thesis contributes to the growing body of research on Retrieval-Augmented Generation 
(RAG) systems by conducting a comparative evaluation of sparse, dense, and hybrid retrieval 
methods in a domain-specific helpdesk context. The work uniquely integrates both automated 
and human evaluation metrics and provides practical insights into how retrieval strategies 
impact factuality, completeness, and clarity of generated responses. Additionally, the study 
draws on expert interviews to shape human-centric evaluation criteria, offering value to both 
academic and enterprise applications of RAG. 
1.5 
Structure 
This thesis is organized into eight chapters, each addressing a specific aspect of the research. 
Chapter 2 provides the background necessary to understand the technical foundations of the 
study, including concepts such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), sparse and dense 
retrieval strategies, and approaches to chatbot evaluation. Chapter 3 presents the research 
methodology, covering the overall research design, the process of collecting qualitative data 
through interviews, and the structure of the model implementation and evaluation procedures. 
Chapter 4 focuses on the technical implementation, detailing the setup of the retrieval 
strategies, the integration of the selected large language models, and the use of a publicly 
available dialogue dataset that simulates customer support interactions. Chapter 5 presents 
the results of the study, including both human evaluation outcomes and quantitative 
performance metrics for each RAG configuration. Chapter 6 offers a critical discussion of the 
findings, examining their implications, potential limitations, and ethical considerations. 
Finally, Chapter 7 concludes the thesis by summarizing key insights and providing 
recommendations for future research in the field of domain-specific conversational AI.

4 
 
2 
Background  
This chapter provides the theoretical and technical foundations necessary to understand the 
implementation and evaluation of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems explored 
in this thesis. The first section introduces key concepts and models used in the study, such as 
large language models, sparse and dense retrieval, and the RAG framework. The second section 
outlines related research and highlights how this work builds upon or differentiates from 
previous efforts in the field. 
2.1 
Preliminaries  
2.1.1 Enterprise Help-Desk Context and Knowledge Bases 
Enterprise help-desk systems handle internal or external support requests related to IT 
systems, services, or products, typically through tickets or live chat. Support agents rely on 
internal knowledge bases (KBs) that contain structured and unstructured resources such as 
troubleshooting guides, FAQs, product documentation, and past case resolutions. 
These knowledge bases pose several challenges: 
• 
High volume and redundancy: Multiple articles may cover similar issues with different 
wording. 
• 
Domain-specific jargon: Users may struggle to phrase queries in technical terms, 
leading to mismatches with stored content. 
• 
Stale or outdated material: Documentation may lag behind product updates. 
• 
Retrieval inefficiency: Traditional keyword-based search often returns irrelevant or 
hard-to-interpret results. 
These limitations reduce the efficiency of support workflows and user satisfaction. 
Consequently, there is growing interest in conversational systems that can retrieve precise, up-
to-date information and present it in natural language. Retrieval-Augmented Generation 
(RAG) offers a promising approach for addressing these challenges in enterprise 
environments. 
2.1.2 Interviews in Empirical Research 
In empirical research, interviews are widely used to collect qualitative data that reflect 
participants' experiences, perceptions, and expectations. They are particularly suitable when 
the goal is to understand human perspectives in depth and when the study seeks to explore 
emerging or underdefined phenomena (Bryman, 2016). Interviews allow researchers to 
uncover insights that may not be captured through quantitative methods alone, especially in 
applied settings such as user experience evaluation or system design. 
This thesis adopts semi-structured interviews with domain experts in technical customer 
support to explore real-world expectations for AI-generated chatbot responses. Semi-
structured interviews offer a balance between consistency across interviews and the flexibility 
to probe deeper into relevant topics that arise during the conversation (Adams, 2015). This 
approach enables richer data collection while maintaining comparability.

5 
 
The findings from these interviews informed the design of the human evaluation framework, 
ensuring that the assessment of chatbot responses reflects end-user priorities such as factual 
accuracy, clarity, and groundedness. Interviews are particularly valuable in this context 
because they bridge the gap between technical implementation and real-world usability. They 
help translate abstract performance metrics into human-centered quality criteria that can be 
used to evaluate conversational AI systems meaningfully (Myers & Newman, 2007). 
2.1.3 Generative AI 
Generative Artificial Intelligence (Generative AI) refers to a class of models capable of 
producing original content such as text, images, audio, or code based on patterns learned from 
large-scale data. In natural language processing (NLP), generative AI systems can compose 
coherent and contextually appropriate responses, making them particularly valuable for 
applications like chatbots, summarization, and question answering. 
At the heart of modern generative AI are foundation models—large-scale pre-trained models 
like GPT, LLaMA, and Mistral which are built using transformer architectures and trained on 
vast corpora of text data (Bommasani et al., 2021). These models exhibit strong generalization 
capabilities, enabling zero-shot or few-shot performance across a wide variety of tasks when 
given appropriate prompts or fine-tuned for specific instructions. 
Despite their strengths, generative models also introduce critical challenges. One major 
limitation is the tendency to hallucinate that is, to generate fluent but incorrect or fabricated 
information, particularly in domains where precise, factual accuracy is essential (Ji et al., 
2023). Additionally, since foundation models are trained on static data, they may lack 
awareness of recent developments or domain-specific terminology. 
In enterprise environments such as help desks, where users expect accurate, grounded, and 
timely responses, these limitations hinder the direct deployment of generative models. To 
address this, approaches like Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) are employed, which 
enhance generative models by grounding them in up-to-date, query-relevant documents. This 
coupling supports domain control, traceability, and factual consistency—key requirements in 
high-stakes or information-sensitive settings.  
2.1.4 Large Language Models (LLMs) 
Large Language Models (LLMs) are deep learning architectures designed to process, 
understand, and generate human language at scale. The majority of modern LLMs are built 
upon the transformer architecture, which employs self-attention mechanisms to capture 
dependencies across an entire text sequence efficiently (Vaswani et al., 2017). The original 
transformer consists of two main components: an encoder, which reads and encodes the input 
sequence, and a decoder, which generates output based on both the encoder’s representation 
and its own previously generated tokens. 
Today’s state-of-the-art LLMs such as LLaMA 2 and Mistral are composed of billions of 
parameters and are pre-trained on massive, diverse datasets that include books, articles, 
websites, and other publicly accessible text sources (Touvron et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2023). 
The transformer architecture has largely supplanted earlier recurrent and convolutional 
models in NLP tasks due to its ability to model long-range relationships and support highly 
parallelized training.

6 
 
In the context of conversational AI, most LLMs use a decoder-only architecture, which 
facilitates efficient left-to-right, autoregressive text generation. This is crucial for producing 
coherent, contextually appropriate responses in dialogue systems. The self-attention 
mechanism at the core of these models is mathematically defined as:

Figure 1 depicts the original transformer architecture, consisting of both encoder and decoder 
components, as introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017). While this full architecture is well-suited 
for sequence-to-sequence tasks such as machine translation, most large language models used 
in conversational AI employ only the decoder portion. This design supports autoregressive 
generation, allowing the model to produce one token at a time in a left-to-right manner based 
on prior context. 
In the figure, the encoder is shown on the left and the decoder on the right. Decoder-only 
architectures have become the standard for generative models due to their efficiency and 
scalability. This architectural choice has enabled LLMs to achieve state-of-the-art results 
across a broad range of natural language processing tasks. Nevertheless, despite these 
advances, LLMs can still generate hallucinated, outdated, or misaligned outputs in enterprise 
settings. These challenges highlight the need for robust adaptation strategies such as retrieval-

7 
 
augmented generation and domain-specific fine-tuning to ensure factual accuracy and 
response reliability (Touvron et al., 2023). 
2.1.5 Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) 
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a hybrid architecture that enhances the generation 
capabilities of large language models (LLMs) by augmenting them with a retrieval component 
that dynamically fetches relevant context from an external knowledge source. Unlike standard 
LLMs that rely solely on pretrained knowledge, RAG provides a mechanism to ground 
responses in current and domain-specific information at inference time, improving factual 
correctness and traceability (Lewis et al., 2020). 
2.1.5.1 Technical Workflow and Architecture 
This section presents the technical foundation of the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) 
system implemented and evaluated in this thesis. The architecture combines information 
retrieval techniques with large language models (LLMs) to generate contextually grounded 
answers based on enterprise knowledge sources. The design is structured to reflect a modular 
pipeline, where each component from query input to response generation plays a specific role 
in ensuring factual accuracy, relevance, and explainability of the output. 
 
 
Figure 2. Overview of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture.                   
Blue-highlighted components represent the focus of this thesis: the retriever and index 
layer, evaluated across sparse (BM25), dense (SBERT+FAISS), and hybrid (RRF) 
strategies. The generator (LLM) and input/output stages are included for completeness but 
are not the primary focus of this study. 
 
Figure 2 illustrates the high-level workflow of the RAG system, highlighting the interaction 
between the retriever, various index types, and the LLM generator. Special attention is given 
to the retriever component, as the thesis primarily focuses on evaluating different retrieval 
strategies and their impact on answer quality. The key modules are described below.

8 
 
1. Query Input: 
The process begins with a natural language input from the user, which represents an 
information need, for example, a technical support question. This input is lightly pre-processed 
(e.g., lowercased and normalized) before being passed to the retriever. 
2. Retriever: 
The retriever is the backbone of the RAG pipeline and the primary focus of this thesis. It is 
responsible for retrieving the most relevant information from a knowledge base to support the 
generation process. Three types of retrievers are compared in this study: 
Sparse Retrieval (BM25) 
Sparse retrieval using BM25 (Best Match 25) remains a foundational method in information 
retrieval, particularly effective in enterprise environments where queries often contain 
domain-specific terminology. BM25 ranks documents based on exact token matches between 
the query and the document, adjusting for term frequency and document length. This makes 
it highly effective for handling queries that include precise keywords or platform-specific 
jargon, which are common in customer support contexts.

Sparse retrieval using BM25 ranks passages based on exact-term matches with TF-IDF-
inspired scoring. It excels in jargon-heavy and multi-keyword queries (Robertson & Zaragoza, 
2009). While BM25 is fast, interpretable, and robust for long, keyword-rich queries, it tends 
to struggle with semantically equivalent phrasings that lack shared vocabulary. 
Dense Retrieval 
Dense retrieval improves on lexical limitations by mapping both queries and documents into a 
shared embedding space using deep neural encoders, typically fine-tuned on sentence-level

9 
 
semantic similarity tasks. Models such as SBERT (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019) and Dense 
Passage Retrieval (Karpukhin et al., 2020) produce dense vectors where semantic similarity is 
reflected in geometric closeness. 
The similarity between a query embedding q and a document embedding d is commonly 
computed using cosine similarity:

Alternatively, if embeddings are L2-normalized, the dot product can be used directly: 
𝑑𝑜𝑡_𝑝𝑟𝑜𝑑𝑢𝑐𝑡(𝒒, 𝒅) = 𝒒⊤𝒅 
These similarity scores are then used to retrieve the top-k most relevant documents through 
approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) indexing algorithms such as FAISS (Johnson et al., 
2017), which enables scalable retrieval through vector quantization, clustering, or graph-based 
search. 
Dense retrieval excels in handling short, vague, or paraphrased queries—often seen in user-
generated content—and generally improves recall in such cases. However, its reliance on 
embedding-based similarity can lead to retrieval of documents that are semantically related 
but contextually misaligned, particularly in domains with fine-grained distinctions between 
concepts. 
Hybrid Retrieval (Sparse + Dense Fusion) 
Hybrid retrieval aims to combine the strengths of both sparse and dense approaches by fusing 
their individual rankings or scores. In this study, we implement Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) 
(Cormack et al., 2009) as a simple yet effective strategy to merge results from BM25 and dense 
retrievers. The RRF score for a document d, given multiple ranked lists R1, R2,..., R𝑚, is defined 
as:

where ranki(𝑑) is the rank of document d in list Ri, and k is a constant (commonly 60) to 
prevent domination by top-ranked results. This formulation ensures that documents 
appearing high in multiple rankings are prioritized, even if their absolute scores differ between 
retrievers. 
Each retriever performs a similarity search using either an inverted index (for sparse) or a 
vector index (for dense), or both (for hybrid). 
3. Index Layer: 
The index layer shown in the figure 2 is the bridge between the knowledge base and the 
retriever. Its goal is to organize content so that relevant evidence can be found quickly and 
reliably at query time.  
Inverted Index (BM25) 
An inverted index maps each token to the list of documents (or spans) that contain it. At query 
time, a lexical scorer such as BM25 combines term frequency, inverse document frequency, 
and soft length normalization to rank candidates. This approach excels when queries include 
domain-specific terminology, product names, or exact phrases common in policy and how-to 
text (Robertson & Zaragoza, 2009).

10 
 
Vector Index (for dense, embedding-based retrieval) 
A vector index stores sentence/paragraph embeddings—continuous representations that place 
semantically related texts near each other. At query time, the system encodes the query into 
the same space and retrieves nearest neighbours by vector similarity (dot product or cosine). 
This approach is effective for paraphrases and short queries where wording differs from the 
source (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019; Karpukhin et al., 2020). Scalable retrieval relies on vector 
search structures that support exact or approximate k-nearest-neighbour search (Johnson et 
al., 2017; Malkov & Yashunin, 2018). Vector indexes provide semantic recall, finding relevant 
evidence even when the user’s language does not match the article’s wording. 
Hybrid retrieval : Using both 
Many RAG systems keep both index types and fuse their ranked lists (e.g., with Reciprocal 
Rank Fusion) to combine lexical precision with semantic recall, improving robustness across 
mixed query styles (Cormack et al., 2009). 
4. Generator (LLM): 
The LLM Generator in a RAG architecture is responsible for producing coherent and 
contextually grounded answers based on the user query and retrieved evidence. Instead of 
relying solely on static, pretrained knowledge, the generator leverages real-time retrieved 
content to improve factual accuracy and reduce hallucination. Any decoder-only, instruction-
tuned large language model can serve this role, such as LLaMA 2 or Mistral 7B Instruct. These 
models generate responses conditioned on the combined input of the user query and retrieved 
passages, allowing the system to deliver more relevant and verifiable answers in enterprise 
settings (Touvron et al., 2023; Vaswani et al., 2017; Bommasani et al., 2021). 
5. Generator Output: 
The Generated Output is the final response produced by the language model, presented to the 
end-user. It combines fluent language generation with factual grounding based on retrieved 
evidence. In RAG systems, this output is typically accompanied by inline citations (e.g., “[1]”, 
“[2]”) that point back to the supporting document snippets, helping users verify the source of 
information. Ensuring that the output is accurate, relevant, complete, and easy to understand 
is crucial in enterprise contexts like customer support. The quality of the generated output 
depends not only on the language model's capabilities but also on the relevance and 
completeness of the retrieved evidence (Lewis et al., 2020; Ji et al., 2023). 
2.1.5.2 Core Libraries and Toolkits for RAG Development 
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems combine multiple components—from 
document indexing to language generation and evaluation. Several open-source libraries play 
a critical role in enabling flexible and scalable RAG implementations. 
Hugging Face Transformers 
The Hugging Face Transformers library (Wolf et al., 2020) provides a unified interface for 
accessing and deploying state-of-the-art transformer-based models for natural language 
processing tasks. It supports a wide range of pretrained models, including encoder-only, 
decoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures, along with utilities for tokenization, text 
generation, and prompt formatting. Its ease of integration and community-driven model hub 
make it a central tool in modern RAG pipelines.

11 
 
Sentence-Transformers 
The Sentence-Transformers library (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019) provides bi-encoder models 
that map sentences or passages into dense vector representations suitable for semantic 
similarity and retrieval tasks. These models are commonly used in dense retrieval pipelines 
where both user queries and documents are embedded into the same vector space for efficient 
comparison. 
FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) 
FAISS is a high-performance library for similarity search and clustering of dense vectors 
(Johnson et al., 2017). It supports both exact and approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search, 
with indexing techniques such as HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World), and FlatL2. 
FAISS is widely used in dense retrieval for enabling low-latency semantic search across large 
document collections (Malkov & Yashunin, 2018). 
General-Purpose Data Processing Tools 
Beyond specialized machine learning and NLP libraries, this work also makes extensive use of 
widely adopted Python libraries for data handling and processing. NumPy and pandas serve 
as the core components for managing tabular data, performing vectorized operations, and 
organizing experimental workflows. These libraries enable efficient loading, transformation, 
and analysis of textual and numerical data at various stages of the RAG pipeline. Their 
flexibility and performance make them essential for preparing inputs to retrievers and models, 
managing intermediate results, and computing evaluation statistics. As a result, they 
contribute significantly to building a transparent, reproducible, and scalable architecture for 
retrieval-augmented generation. 
2.1.5.3 Advantages and Limitations of RAG 
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) offers a compelling architecture that combines the 
generative flexibility of large language models (LLMs) with the precision and factual grounding 
of retrieval-based systems. One of its key strengths lies in its ability to generate contextually 
relevant and up-to-date responses by accessing external knowledge sources at inference time. 
This makes RAG particularly effective for domain-specific applications, such as enterprise help 
desks, where information needs to be accurate, traceable, and aligned with dynamic knowledge 
bases. By incorporating retrieval steps, RAG also mitigates the hallucination problem 
commonly observed in standalone LLMs, increasing factual correctness and supporting 
answer verifiability (Lewis et al., 2020; Ji et al., 2023). 
However, RAG systems also introduce several challenges. Their performance is highly 
dependent on the quality and relevance of retrieved passages, which in turn hinges on the 
retrieval strategy (sparse, dense, or hybrid), chunking method, and index configuration. 
Suboptimal retrieval can lead to incomplete or misleading responses, even if the language 
model itself is capable. Furthermore, RAG adds architectural complexity, requiring the 
orchestration of multiple components retrievers, indexes, context selectors, and LLMs which 
can increase latency, system maintenance, and infrastructure requirements. Ensuring citation 
accuracy, handling contradictory evidence, and maintaining interpretability in real-world 
deployments remain active areas of research and practical concern.

12 
 
2.1.6 Evaluation Strategies: Retrieval and Generation Quality 
Evaluating the performance of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems requires 
assessing both the quality of the retrieved content and the generated responses. To 
comprehensively assess our RAG configurations, we employed a combination of automated 
metrics for retrieval and generation quality, as well as structured human evaluations 
performed by domain experts and end users. This two-pronged approach enables us to capture 
both the objective performance characteristics and subjective user satisfaction—both of which 
are critical for real-world deployment in enterprise environments (Liu et al., 2016; Feldman et 
al., 2024). 
2.1.6.1 Automated Evaluation Metrics 
Automated evaluation provides a scalable and reproducible method to compare different 
system configurations using well-established metrics. In this study, automated evaluation was 
applied at two levels: 
• 
Retrieval Quality: To assess how well the retriever surfaced relevant knowledge. 
• 
Generation Quality: To measure how well the generated answers aligned with reference 
outputs in both content and semantic similarity.

The use of nDCG in IR and LLM evaluation is well-established in both academic and industry

2.1.6.2 Human Evaluation 
While automated metrics offer objective and scalable evaluation of language model outputs, 
they often fall short in capturing qualitative aspects of communication—such as nuance, 
contextual relevance, and user satisfaction—that are vital in enterprise applications (Liu et al., 
2016; Mousavi et al., 2022; Feldman et al., 2024). In customer support contexts, especially 
those involving domain-specific knowledge, human assessment plays an essential 
complementary role. 
Evaluator Composition and Setup 
This study employed a structured human evaluation involving five participants: three domain 
experts (support specialists from the case company) and two end users with real-world 
familiarity with the product domain. Notably, the four evaluation criteria—Factual 
Correctness, Relevance, Clarity, and Completeness—were informed directly by prior semi-
structured interviews with the support experts, ensuring that the evaluation dimensions 
aligned with business needs and user expectations.

15 
 
Each evaluator received ten user queries along with six generated responses per query, drawn 
from three retrieval configurations (sparse, dense, hybrid) combined with two open LLMs 
(LLaMA-2 and Mistral). To support evaluators in making fair and consistent judgments, a 
ground-truth reference answer was provided for each query. This allowed direct comparison 
between the expected content and the generated outputs. 
Evaluation Protocol and Scoring Guidelines 
Evaluators independently rated each response on a 1–5 Likert scale across the four defined 
dimensions. Fractional scores (e.g., 4.5) were allowed to capture nuance. The following 
guidelines were shared to calibrate the scoring process: 
Factual Correctness: 
Assesses whether the response is factually accurate, without hallucinations or misleading 
statements. 
5 (Excellent): Entirely accurate and fact-based, with no errors. 
4 (Good): Mostly accurate, minor omissions or small issues that don’t affect the message. 
3 (Fair): Some factual mistakes or oversimplifications, but the core is correct. 
2 (Poor): Significant inaccuracies or misleading information. 
1 (Very Poor): Factually incorrect, hallucinated, or harmful information. 
Relevance 
Measures how well the response addresses the user’s question using context from the 
retrievals. 
5 (Excellent): Fully answers the question, stays on topic, and uses the provided evidence. 
4 (Good): Mostly relevant, minor digressions or missing secondary aspects. 
3 (Fair): Partially relevant, includes off-topic content or misses important subtopics. 
2 (Poor): Barely relevant or largely misses the intent of the question. 
1 (Very Poor): Completely irrelevant or nonsensical. 
Clarity 
Evaluates how well the response is written in terms of grammar, structure, and readability. 
5 (Excellent): Clear, grammatically correct, well-structured, and easy to understand. 
4 (Good): Mostly fluent, with minor grammar or phrasing issues. 
3 (Fair): Some awkward phrasing or unclear sentences, but understandable. 
2 (Poor): Disjointed or confusing due to poor grammar or structure. 
1 (Very Poor): Incomprehensible or very poorly written. 
Completeness 
Assesses how thoroughly the response answers the user's question, including necessary details. 
5 (Excellent): Fully complete and covers all aspects with relevant context or examples. 
4 (Good): Covers most parts, with minor omissions. 
3 (Fair): Partially complete, misses important details or only covers part of the query.

16 
 
2 (Poor): Lacks depth, missing key information. 
1 (Very Poor): Highly incomplete or uninformative. 
A human evaluation was conducted to complement automated metrics and assess how well 
each RAG configuration aligns with user expectations. A group of five evaluators – two domain 
experts and three end-users – were involved in the assessment. They rated the generated 
responses on four dimensions: Factuality, Relevance, Clarity, and Completeness using a 1–5 
Likert scale. 
While no inter-rater reliability metrics (such as Krippendorff’s Alpha or Cohen’s Kappa) were 
computed, this decision was based on two practical considerations. First, the evaluation was 
conducted in a live workshop setting, where anonymized individual scores were not retained 
after aggregation due to privacy and consent constraints aligned with GDPR. Second, the 
primary aim of the evaluation was not to measure rater agreement statistically, but to reflect a 
combined judgment of expert and end-user expectations in a real-world enterprise setting. To 
mitigate individual bias, scores were averaged across five raters using a predefined rubric to 
maintain consistency. 
This expert and user informed human assessment protocol enables a rich, multi-perspective 
evaluation of generated responses, complementing automated metrics and ensuring that 
model outputs are not only technically correct, but also usable, understandable, and relevant 
in real-world enterprise contexts (Mousavi et al., 2022; Feldman et al., 2024). 
2.2 
Research background  
This section reviews recent work on retrieval strategies in Retrieval-Augmented Generation 
(RAG) systems, particularly in the context of domain-specific question answering and 
conversational AI. It highlights findings on sparse, dense, and hybrid retrieval, identifies 
shortcomings in current comparative studies, and clarifies how this thesis addresses these gaps 
through systematic human and automated evaluation using open-source models and datasets. 
2.2.1 Sparse vs. Dense Retrieval in RAG: Karpukhin et al. (2020) 
and Maillard et al. (2021) 
Karpukhin et al. (2020) introduced Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR), a dual-encoder 
architecture that embeds queries and passages in a shared vector space for semantic retrieval. 
In comparative experiments, DPR significantly outperformed the BM25 baseline on open-
domain QA tasks, achieving 9%–19% absolute improvements in Top-20 passage retrieval 
accuracy. Additionally, their end-to-end QA system surpassed previous benchmarks, reporting 
41.5% Exact Match (EM) on the Natural Questions dataset compared to 33.3% for ORQA. 
These results demonstrate the substantial retrieval and downstream QA gains that can be 
achieved with dense methods over traditional sparse retrievers. 
Maillard et al. (2021) extended the comparison between sparse and dense retrieval methods in 
multi-hop question answering. They found that sparse retrievers like BM25 perform well when 
queries closely match the document language, while dense models are more robust to 
paraphrased or abstract queries. Their work emphasized the importance of query formulation 
on retrieval effectiveness, particularly in complex multi-hop settings, but did not evaluate 
hybrid retrieval configurations.

17 
 
Relevance: These studies support the hypothesis that dense retrieval models can offer 
substantial performance advantages over sparse baselines in many QA settings. However, they 
primarily evaluate retrieval accuracy and end-to-end QA in open-domain datasets, without 
exploring hybrid methods or applying human evaluation. This thesis extends their work by 
comparing sparse, dense, and hybrid retrieval strategies in an enterprise help-desk context, 
using both automated and structured human evaluation to assess downstream response 
quality. 
2.2.2 Dense Retrieval in Industrial Applications: Hofstätter et al. 
(2021) 
Hofstätter et al. (2021) evaluated dense retrieval models (e.g., ColBERT and BERT-based dual 
encoders) for customer support QA tasks using large-scale datasets such as MS MARCO and 
TREC-DL. Their work emphasized the trade-off between retrieval effectiveness and real-world 
deployment feasibility, noting that dense retrieval provides superior semantic matching but 
often demands considerable infrastructure for vector indexing and approximate nearest 
neighbor (ANN) search. As a strong sparse retrieval baseline, BM25 achieved nDCG@10 = 
0.501 and MRR@10 = 0.689 on TREC-DL 2019, nDCG@10 = 0.475 and MRR@10 = 0.649 on 
TREC-DL 2020, and nDCG@10 = 0.241 and MRR@10 = 0.194 on the MS MARCO dev set. 
Dense retrieval models trained with their TAS-balanced sampling method significantly 
outperformed BM25 on all benchmarks (p < 0.05), demonstrating the value of dense 
representations in capturing semantic relevance. 
Relevance: Although the work by Hofstätter et al. provides compelling evidence of dense 
retrieval’s superiority over BM25 in terms of automated metrics, it does not extend the 
evaluation to a full RAG pipeline, nor does it incorporate human assessments or compare with 
hybrid methods. The present thesis builds on these insights by systematically comparing dense 
and hybrid retrievers within an end-to-end RAG framework, using both automated and 
human-centered evaluation to assess overall system quality. 
2.2.3 Hybrid Retrieval with Reciprocal Rank Fusion: Cormack et al. 
(2009) and Oguz et al. (2022) 
Cormack et al. (2009) proposed Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), a simple yet robust method 
for combining ranked lists from multiple retrieval systems. RRF has since been widely adopted 
for hybrid retrieval due to its ability to balance sparse and dense signals. Oguz et al. (2022) 
applied RRF in multi-stage retrieval systems and demonstrated its effectiveness across various 
TREC (Text Retrieval Conference) tracks. 
Relevance: While RRF is known to improve top-k retrieval recall, especially when combining 
sparse and dense models, few studies evaluate its impact within conversational RAG pipelines 
or on generated response quality. This thesis explicitly implements and evaluates RRF-based 
hybrid retrieval in an enterprise QA context. While retrieval strategies have been extensively 
benchmarked, much less attention has been given to evaluating the quality of generated 
responses within RAG systems. This gap highlights the importance of human-centered 
evaluation, which is addressed in the following section.

18 
 
2.2.4 Human-Centered Evaluation of RAG Responses: Mousavi et 
al. (2022) and Feldman et al. (2024) 
Mousavi et al. (2022) emphasized the need for shareable and replicable evaluation protocols 
in generative QA, proposing a multi-dimensional human evaluation framework including 
clarity, informativeness, and factuality. Feldman et al. (2024) expanded on this by 
demonstrating how domain experts' judgments can reveal quality issues missed by automated 
metrics like ROUGE or BERTScore. They argued for the necessity of combining expert and 
end-user perspectives when evaluating task-specific generation systems. 
Relevance: These studies reinforce the limitations of automated metrics and validate our 
approach of using structured human evaluations based on real stakeholder interviews. This 
thesis extends this by evaluating four quality dimensions—factual correctness, relevance, 
clarity, and completeness—based on practitioner expectations in customer support. 
2.2.5 Automated Evaluation Benchmarks in RAG: Chen et al. (2024) 
Chen et al. (2024) introduced BSharedRAG, a framework that explores the impact of 
backbone-sharing between the retriever and generator in Retrieval-Augmented Generation 
(RAG) systems. The authors conducted extensive evaluations in the e-commerce domain using 
both retrieval and generation metrics. Their experiments compared various retriever-
generator combinations (e.g., Baichuan2-7B, GPT-4, EcomGPT) across public benchmarks 
such as CPR-Ecom and WorthBuying, reporting nDCG@3, nDCG@5, and Hit@K for retrieval, 
and ROUGE-L, BLEU-3, and BERTScore for generation. 
Their baseline results show that non-shared fully independent models like "FullySharedRAG" 
scored relatively lower across all metrics, with ROUGE-L of 2.58, BLEU-3 of 2.35, and 
BERTScore of 62.37. In contrast, separated retrievers like LLaMA3-based achieved ROUGE-L 
of 10.38, BLEU-3 of 10.35, and BERTScore of 71.84. Similarly, for retrieval performance on 
CPR-Ecom, the BGE-large-zh + HN model achieved nDCG@5 = 56.23 and Hit@5 = 68.50, 
significantly outperforming the baseline FullySharedRAG (nDCG@5 = 43.24, Hit@5 = 48.03). 
Relevance: This paper is notable for reporting detailed benchmark values across widely-used 
automated metrics in RAG systems, serving as a strong reference for comparative evaluation. 
Unlike many prior studies that only discuss relative improvements, it provides concrete 
numerical baselines across retriever and generator combinations. Our thesis builds upon this 
trend by similarly reporting Hit@K, nDCG@K, ROUGE-L, BERTScore, and Cosine Similarity 
in an enterprise helpdesk context. However, in contrast to Chen et al., we complement these 
automated metrics with structured human evaluation on factuality, relevance, clarity, and 
completeness—an area often underexplored in purely metric-driven studies. 
2.2.6 Gaps in the Literature and Contribution of This Thesis 
Although Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has shown promise for improving the 
factual grounding of large language models (LLMs), much of the existing research has 
emphasized the generative component while treating the retriever as a fixed module. 
Systematic comparisons of different retrieval strategies within RAG remain limited, and the 
implications of retrieval design choices on downstream response quality are not yet well 
understood.

19 
 
Furthermore, many prior studies rely on proprietary datasets or closed-source models, which 
makes it difficult to reproduce findings or transfer insights to practical enterprise settings. 
Evaluation frameworks that integrate both retrieval and generation quality are still rare, and 
few studies incorporate structured human assessments to complement automated metrics. 
This thesis addresses these gaps by placing the retriever at the center of evaluation. It 
systematically compares three retrieval strategies—sparse retrieval using BM25, dense 
retrieval using SBERT with FAISS, and hybrid retrieval using Reciprocal Rank Fusion—
combined with two open-source LLMs (LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct). In 
addition to automated evaluation, a human study involving both domain specialists and end-
users was conducted to assess the quality of generated responses along criteria such as factual 
correctness, relevance, clarity, and completeness. 
By combining open-source models, a publicly available dataset (Doc2Dial), and a unified 
evaluation methodology, this thesis contributes a retriever-aware benchmark for RAG 
pipelines in enterprise help-desk scenarios. The findings provide actionable insights into how 
retrieval design choices affect both quantitative performance and perceived response quality, 
offering guidance for building reliable and context-sensitive conversational AI systems. 
3 
Method 
This chapter outlines the methodological approach taken to investigate the effectiveness of 
various retrieval strategies in a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework applied to 
domain-specific conversational AI. The study integrates both qualitative and quantitative 
methods to ensure a comprehensive evaluation of retrieval and generation performance. It 
details the research design, data sources, model implementations, evaluation protocols, and 
considerations for validity and reliability. By combining real-world user input with controlled 
experimentation, this methodology aims to bridge the gap between theoretical performance 
and practical usability of LLM-based support systems. 
3.1 
Research Design 
This thesis adopts a mixed-methods research design that combines qualitative user interviews 
with a structured, quantitative evaluation of large language models (LLMs) under different 
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) configurations. The aim is to systematically assess 
how different retrieval strategies influence the quality of generated responses in a domain-
specific conversational AI setting. 
The qualitative component consists of semi-structured interviews with three domain support 
experts. These interviews were conducted to understand real-world user expectations 
regarding AI-generated answers and to derive a set of human evaluation criteria grounded in 
practical enterprise needs. The insights gathered from these interviews informed the design of 
the human assessment rubric used later in the study. This qualitative approach captures 
subjective, human-centered aspects of system performance, such as perceived clarity, factual 
correctness, and completeness. 
The quantitative component, also referred to as automated evaluation, involves a controlled 
experimental assessment of RAG pipelines using three retrieval strategies: Sparse Retrieval 
(BM25), Dense Retrieval (SBERT + FAISS), and Hybrid Retrieval (Reciprocal Rank Fusion). 
These retrieval methods were paired with two open-source decoder-only LLMs (LLaMA-2-7B

20 
 
and Mistral-7B) and evaluated on the Doc2Dial dataset, which simulates realistic enterprise 
dialogue grounded in documentation. Performance was measured using standard automated 
metrics: for retrieval, Hit@K, MRR, and nDCG@K; and for generation, ROUGE-L, BERTScore 
F1, and SBERT cosine similarity. These metrics offer objective, reproducible insights into the 
technical performance of different configurations. 
By integrating both quantitative (automated) and qualitative (human-centered) methods, this 
research provides a holistic view of the trade-offs involved in selecting retrieval strategies for 
RAG systems. The design ensures technical rigor through reproducible experiments and 
established benchmarks, while maintaining contextual relevance by grounding evaluation 
criteria in domain expert expectations. This approach supports the broader research goal of 
identifying retrieval strategies that optimize both factual accuracy and user-perceived quality 
in real-world help-desk scenarios. 
3.2 
Data Collection 
This study involves two key data collection activities: (1) gathering real-world expectations 
from domain experts through interviews, and (2) preparing a benchmark dataset for evaluating 
retrieval and generation in a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline. 
3.2.1 Expert Interviews for Human Evaluation Criteria 
To ensure that the human evaluation framework reflects actual user needs, semi-structured 
interviews were conducted with three domain support specialists working in the IT support 
unit of a mining machinery manufacturing company. These individuals were selected based on 
their hands-on experience in resolving technical queries from internal and external users, 
making them ideal informants for understanding user expectations in an enterprise help-desk 
context. 
The interviews were conducted individually, recorded with consent, and later transcribed. 
Each interview followed a predefined set of questions focused on expectations around factual 
accuracy, answer relevance, clarity, completeness, and preferences for citation or traceability. 
The interviews also probed how users would judge the helpfulness or correctness of an AI-
generated response in a real support situation. 
The qualitative insights from these interviews were thematically analysed and directly shaped 
the design of the human evaluation rubric, consisting of four criteria: Factual Correctness, 
Relevance, Clarity, and Completeness. These criteria were later used by the same experts, along 
with two additional end users, to evaluate generated responses in a workshop setting.  
The summary of the meeting transcripts is provided in Appendix A. To protect participant 
privacy, no video or audio recordings were retained. Instead, participants gave explicit consent 
during the Teams sessions to allow note-taking and text transcription. This consent remains 
documented within the company’s Teams system. Only anonymized summaries of the 
interviews are included in this thesis to ensure compliance with ethical and data protection 
requirements. 
These interviews directly informed the construction of the human evaluation rubric used later 
in the study (see Section 5.2).

21 
 
3.2.2 Dataset for Retrieval and Generation Evaluation 
This study employs the Doc2Dial dataset—a goal-oriented, document-grounded dialogue 
benchmark introduced by Feng et al. (2020)—to evaluate the retrieval and generation 
components of the RAG pipeline. Doc2Dial consists of approximately 4,800 annotated 
dialogues, each averaging 14 conversational turns and grounded in over 480 documents across 
four diverse. 
The dataset was intentionally selected due to its close alignment with enterprise help-desk 
scenarios. It realistically simulates information-seeking conversations within structured 
documents, such as FAQs or user manuals. Each dialogue sample includes the conversation 
history, a user query, and annotated grounding spans within the associated document—either 
as solution or condition—which provides a strong foundation for evaluating retrieval strategies 
and subsequent generation quality. 
Doc2Dial supports two key modelling tasks: 
Knowledge Identification: Selecting the relevant text span in the document based on the 
conversation context. 
Response Generation: Generating the agent's next utterance in natural language, grounded in 
the document context. 
These structured tasks make Doc2Dial particularly suitable for evaluating both the retriever's 
effectiveness (e.g., Hit@K, MRR, nDCG) and the generation output using automatic metrics 
and human evaluation in a RAG setting. 
3.2.3 Model Implementation 
The Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system developed for this study was designed to 
investigate the impact of different retrieval strategies on the quality of responses generated by 
large language models (LLMs) in a domain-specific helpdesk context. The entire 
implementation was built using open-source tools, and experiments were conducted on the 
Doc2Dial dataset, which offers a realistic benchmark for document-grounded, goal-oriented 
dialogues in enterprise scenarios (Feng et al., 2021). 
Two open-source decoder-only LLMs were used for the generation step: LLaMA 2 7B Chat and 
Mistral 7B Instruct. These models were selected to reflect strong representatives of open-
weight models with conversational capabilities. The models were deployed locally using the 
vLLM inference engine, which enables efficient and scalable generation through paged 
attention. The models were accessed through the transformers library, and responses were 
generated deterministically (with temperature set to 0.0) to ensure consistent and repeatable 
output across different retrieval setups. Temperature is a parameter that controls the 
randomness of output generation; a value of 0.0 ensures fully deterministic results, where the 
model always chooses the most probable next token. 
To evaluate retrieval strategy effects independently of model behaviour, both LLMs were used 
in conjunction with three retrieval configurations: sparse, dense, and hybrid. The sparse 
retrieval setup was implemented using the BM25 algorithm via the rank_bm25 Python library, 
indexing the passages based on term frequency-inverse document frequency. For dense 
retrieval, semantic vectors were generated using the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 Sentence-BERT model 
and indexed using FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) to enable fast approximate nearest-

22 
 
neighbour search in high-dimensional space. Finally, a hybrid approach used Reciprocal Rank 
Fusion (RRF) to combine and re-rank results from both the sparse and dense retrievers, aiming 
to harness the strengths of lexical precision and semantic generalization. All retrieval strategies 
were implemented manually in Python to ensure transparency and flexibility, rather than 
relying on pre-packaged black-box libraries like Pyserini. 
Following retrieval, the top-k evidence snippets were cleaned and numbered to eliminate 
formatting inconsistencies and emphasize key content. These snippets were then included in 
the final prompt presented to the LLM, along with the original user query. The prompt 
included a strict instruction to base the response only on the provided context, and to reference 
the numbered snippets using citation markers (e.g., [1], [2]). This grounded prompting setup 
was held constant across all configurations to facilitate fair comparison. 
For each query, the generated response and its corresponding retrieval set were logged, along 
with metadata needed for downstream analysis. While no experiment-tracking platforms such 
as MLflow were used, manual reproducibility was ensured through a combination of 
configuration scripts, checkpointing, and systematic logging practices. This consistent and 
transparent setup allowed for thorough automated and human evaluation across different 
combinations of retrieval methods and LLMs. 
3.2.3.1 Computational Environment and Trials 
All experiments were conducted using Google Colab Pro with GPU acceleration. Depending on 
availability, NVIDIA A100, T4, or L4 GPUs were used, providing up to 40 GB GPU RAM, 83.5 
GB system RAM, and approximately 235 GB disk space. To fit within these constraints, 4-bit 
NF4 quantization was applied to both LLaMA 2 and Mistral models during inference. 
In total, three experimental trials were conducted across six system implementations (three 
retrieval strategies × two LLMs). Each configuration was executed deterministically 
(temperature = 0.0) to ensure reproducibility of outputs. The complete project consumed 
approximately 90 GPU compute units out of an allocated 100, distributed across retriever 
evaluation, automated generation testing, and human evaluation preparation. 
3.2.4 Evaluation Method 
The evaluation of the RAG configurations in this study was conducted using a dual approach, 
combining automated metrics and structured human assessments to provide a comprehensive 
understanding of response quality. This multi-perspective evaluation was critical to capturing 
both the quantitative performance of the system and the qualitative attributes that matter in 
real-world enterprise settings. 
The automated evaluation was split into two parts: one focused on the retriever performance, 
and the other on the generation quality. For retrievers, we computed several ranking metrics 
widely used in information retrieval: Hit@K (where K = 1, 3, 5, 10), Mean Reciprocal Rank 
(MRR), and Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG@K). These metrics evaluate how 
well the retriever returns relevant passages within the top-ranked results, with higher scores 
indicating more precise retrieval. The relevance of retrieved passages was determined by 
whether they contained the ground truth answers from the Doc2Dial dataset. 
For generated responses, three text quality metrics were used: ROUGE-L, BERTScore, and 
Sentence-BERT Cosine Similarity. ROUGE-L captures surface-level overlap using the longest

23 
 
common subsequence between the generated and reference texts (Lin, 2004). BERTScore uses 
contextual embeddings to assess semantic similarity between the candidate and reference 
responses (Zhang et al., 2019). Sentence-BERT cosine similarity measures how close the 
sentence embeddings of the model’s response are to the ground truth, offering an additional 
layer of semantic validation (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019). 
In addition to automated evaluation, a human evaluation was conducted to assess more 
nuanced aspects of response quality, such as factual correctness, clarity, and completeness. To 
design the human evaluation criteria, we conducted interviews with three domain experts 
(support specialists), gathering their expectations and preferences. The final evaluation was 
carried out in a structured workshop involving five evaluators—the three support experts and 
two end users. Each evaluator reviewed 10 generated responses from six different RAG 
configurations (3 retrieval methods × 2 LLMs), scoring them on four criteria: Factual 
Correctness, Relevance, Clarity, and Completeness. A five-point Likert scale was used (1 = Very 
Poor, 5 = Excellent), with guidelines provided to ensure consistency. Ground truth answers 
were also supplied to aid evaluators in making objective judgments. 
The average score across all evaluators was considered the final score per response, helping to 
mitigate individual bias. No inter-rater reliability statistics were calculated; however, the 
group-based evaluation structure helped maintain balance and validity across assessments. By 
combining retrieval accuracy, semantic similarity, and expert judgment, this evaluation 
strategy provides both depth and breadth in assessing the performance of RAG configurations 
in a domain-specific conversational AI setting. 
3.2.5 Human evaluation protocol 
The human evaluation was designed to complement automated metrics with human 
judgments of response quality in a domain-specific help-desk setting. Its primary aim was to 
assess generated answers across four dimensions (Factual Correctness, Relevance, Clarity, 
Completeness) for the six RAG configurations evaluated in the study. 
3.2.5.1 Participants 
Five evaluators participated in the study, consisting of three domain experts and two end-
users. All participants were invited from the organisation, volunteered to participate, and their 
involvement was formally approved by management. Each evaluator had more than ten years 
of industrial experience. The domain experts worked as object specialists in technical support, 
while the end-users were engineers who were familiar with the documentation typically used 
in enterprise settings. This composition provided a balance between expert judgment and 
practical user perspectives. 
3.2.5.2 Materials 
The evaluation was based on ten curated and representative queries drawn from the Doc2Dial 
validation set. For each query, six responses were generated, corresponding to the different 
combinations of retrieval strategy and language model (3 retrievers × 2 LLMs). In total, each 
participant evaluated sixty responses. An Excel-based evaluation template was provided to the 
participants, which contained the four assessment criteria on a five-point Likert scale, as well 
as detailed rubrics and written guidelines to calibrate scoring. The completed templates were 
returned to the researcher and served as the primary data source for aggregation and analysis.

24 
 
3.2.5.3 Procedure 
At the start of the evaluation, the rubric and scoring guidelines were explained to the 
participants in a dedicated briefing session. Clear instructions were provided on how to use the 
template and how to interpret each of the four evaluation dimensions, and any questions raised 
by the evaluators were addressed before beginning. Each participant then reviewed sixty 
responses in total (ten queries with six outputs each), scoring every response along the four 
rubric dimensions. The retrieved evidence snippets associated with each query were also made 
available, and evaluators were instructed to consult them before assigning a score. 
The evaluation used a five-point Likert scale (1 = very poor, 5 = excellent). However, evaluators 
were not restricted to whole numbers and were allowed to assign fractional values (e.g., 2.3, 
4.7). This approach was chosen deliberately to capture more fine-grained judgments, as the 
number of evaluators was limited and it was important to reflect subtle differences in perceived 
quality. Allowing fractional scores helped ensure that the aggregated results better represented 
evaluators’ nuanced assessments rather than forcing them into coarser categories. 
The responses from domain specialists and end-users were collected separately in order to 
later examine potential group-level differences. The analysis of these differences is presented 
in the results section (see Section 5.2). 
The evaluation was designed as a single session lasting approximately 1.5 hours, during which 
all responses could be assessed comfortably. On average, evaluators completed the task within 
the allocated time. To mitigate potential bias from order effects, responses were presented in 
mixed order. Where feasible, presentation order was randomized across evaluators. All scores 
were recorded in the Excel templates, and the completed templates were collected and 
aggregated for analysis. 
3.2.5.4 Measures and Analysis Plan 
The primary measures consisted of per-response ratings for factual correctness, relevance, 
clarity, and completeness. After collection, the scores were aggregated by computing means 
across all five evaluators. Separate averages were also calculated for domain specialists and 
end-users to detect potential systematic differences between groups. The analysis showed that 
the differences were minimal, which suggests that criteria derived from expert interviews did 
not introduce meaningful bias. A detailed presentation of the aggregated results is provided in 
Section 5.2. In line with the evaluation design, participants were allowed to use fractional 
scores (e.g., 2.3, 4.7) rather than only whole numbers on the five-point scale. This decision was 
motivated by the relatively small number of evaluators, where fine-grained scoring provides a 
more sensitive reflection of subjective judgments. The use of fractional values reduced the loss 
of information that occurs when nuanced perceptions are forced into coarse integer categories, 
and it allowed the aggregated means to better represent subtle differences across 
configurations. 
Formal inter-rater reliability metrics were not computed due to privacy constraints, as only 
anonymized aggregated scores were retained. Instead, reliability was supported by the use of 
detailed rubrics, anonymized evaluation, and averaging across raters to minimize individual 
variability. This procedure aligns with recommended practices for human-centered evaluation 
of generative question-answering systems, where small groups of domain experts and end-
users provide structured qualitative assessments that complement automated metrics 
(Mousavi et al., 2022; Feldman et al., 2024).

25 
 
3.2.5.5 Ethical Considerations and Data Handling 
The study was conducted in accordance with ethical and data protection guidelines. Informed 
consent was obtained from all participants, and no personally identifiable information was 
retained. Audio or video recordings were not stored; instead, anonymized summaries of the 
evaluation sessions and the completed scoring templates were archived. This approach 
minimized privacy risks while ensuring reproducibility. Consent records are securely stored in 
the company system, and anonymized summary of transcripts are included in the appendices 
of this thesis. 
3.2.6 Validity and Reliability 
Establishing the validity and reliability of the research is crucial for ensuring the 
trustworthiness and credibility of the findings. In this study, a combination of empirical 
implementation, automated metrics, and structured human evaluation was used to rigorously 
assess the quality of RAG-based responses. Each step in the method was designed with 
attention to both internal and external validity, as well as the consistency and reproducibility 
of the results. 
Validity 
In the context of this thesis, validity refers to how accurately the methods and evaluation 
metrics measure what they are intended to measure. To ensure content validity, the evaluation 
criteria for human assessments were not arbitrarily chosen; they were derived from interviews 
with three experienced support specialists working in an enterprise help-desk setting. These 
interviews helped define practical and domain-relevant expectations for factual correctness, 
relevance, clarity, and completeness — ensuring that human evaluations are grounded in real-
world requirements rather than abstract academic criteria. 
Construct validity was supported by the use of widely accepted automated evaluation metrics, 
including ROUGE-L, BERTScore, and Cosine Similarity for generated output, and Hit@k, 
MRR, and nDCG@k for retrieval performance. These metrics are standard in the NLP and 
information retrieval literature, and they align with the theoretical constructs of linguistic 
quality, semantic alignment, and retrieval precision, respectively. 
External validity was addressed by using a publicly available dataset (Doc2Dial), which enables 
other researchers to replicate the experiments and evaluate whether similar outcomes can be 
observed in related domains. The use of two different LLMs — Mistral 7B and LLaMA 2 7B — 
further strengthens generalizability by evaluating RAG performance across models with 
different architectural characteristics. 
Reliability 
Reliability in this context refers to the consistency and stability of the evaluation process. For 
automated metrics, reliability is inherently high due to the deterministic nature of the scoring 
algorithms. For human evaluation, consistency was ensured through a structured workshop 
where five evaluators (three domain specialists and two end users) assessed the same set of 
outputs under standardized guidelines. 
As discussed in Section 2.1.6.2, inter-rater reliability metrics were not computed due to privacy 
and design constraints. However, all evaluators followed a predefined rubric, and the final 
score for each response was calculated as the average of all ratings to minimize individual bias. 
The entire evaluation process was logged and documented to support transparency and

26 
reproducibility. 
Furthermore, implementation and evaluation steps were coded and version-controlled, 
making the pipeline replicable and traceable. Tools such as Colab notebooks and Hugging 
Face’s ecosystem helped in maintaining consistency in model inference, retrieval operations, 
and result storage. 
3.2.7 Methodological Reflection 
The methodological choices in this study were grounded in the goal of evaluating Retrieval-
Augmented Generation (RAG) systems in a practical, enterprise-like setting with a strong 
emphasis on both retrieval and generation quality. A mixed-method approach combining 
automated metrics and human evaluation allowed for a well-rounded assessment of system 
performance. 
The selection of the Doc2Dial dataset ensured realism and relevance, as it simulates multi-turn 
dialogue grounded in domain-specific documentation, reflecting real-world help-desk 
scenarios. The use of open-source models (Mistral 7B and LLaMA 2 7B) supported 
transparency and reproducibility, while also enabling fair benchmarking across multiple 
retrieval strategies These models come with inherent limitations, such as the absence of fine-
tuning on the target domain, which may affect stylistic alignment or clarity in some cases. 
However, this omission was a deliberate methodological choice. Fine-tuning was excluded for 
three reasons: (1) to isolate and evaluate the impact of retrieval strategies without introducing 
confounding effects from model adaptation; (2) because the selected models (Mistral-7B-
Instruct and LLaMA-2-7B-Chat) are already instruction-tuned and well-suited for grounded 
prompting tasks; and (3) due to computational and reproducibility constraints, as fine-tuning 
large models typically requires extensive resources and hyperparameter tuning. Future 
research could extend this work by incorporating task-specific fine-tuning or reinforcement 
learning with human feedback (RLHF) to further improve domain clarity and style. 
Using interviews to derive evaluation criteria proved highly effective in capturing real-world 
expectations, but it also introduced subjectivity, as preferences and interpretations varied 
slightly among evaluators. Although inter-rater agreement was not formally measured, 
averaging scores across five evaluators helped to reduce individual bias and increase reliability. 
On the retrieval side, implementing three strategies — sparse (BM25), dense (SBERT+FAISS), 
and hybrid (RRF) — allowed for an exploration of the trade-offs between precision, latency, 
and context relevance. The RAG pipeline was designed to be modular and interpretable, which 
adds to its practical value for enterprise use cases. However, retriever optimization (e.g., query 
expansion or relevance feedback) was not explored in depth and remains a potential area for 
future work. 
Overall, the methods adopted in this thesis offer a credible and replicable framework for 
evaluating domain-specific conversational AI systems. While some methodological choices 
(e.g., lack of inter-rater reliability scoring, limited fine-tuning) reflect resource constraints, the 
study balances rigor and feasibility in a way that is appropriate for applied research and 
industrial contexts.

This chapter describes the end-to-end implementation of the Retrieval-Augmented Generation 
(RAG) framework used in this thesis. The process includes dataset preparation, retriever 
construction, language model integration, prompt-based generation, and evaluation using 
both automated metrics and human assessments. Figure 3 below illustrates the overall 
workflow, highlighting the different retriever–generator combinations and the parallel 
evaluation pathways employed to benchmark performance.

4.1 
Dataset Preparation  
The dataset used in this thesis is Doc2Dial, a public benchmark designed for modelling 
information-seeking dialogues grounded in document collections (Feng et al., 2021). The 
dataset contains two primary components: a document file (doc2dial_doc.json) and a dialogue 
file (doc2dial_dial_validation.json). The document file includes structured documents 
segmented into spans, each associated with a title and hierarchical context (e.g., parent section 
titles), while the dialogue file contains multi-turn interactions between users and agents. In 
this study, the validation split was used exclusively, with no model fine-tuning applied; all 
indices were built over the provided validation documents. 
To prepare the data for retrieval and generation, several preprocessing steps were performed. 
First, a span-level corpus was constructed where each document span was enriched by 
concatenating the span_text with its corresponding title and parent_titles. In cases where the 
span_text was too short, the text_sec field was appended as a fallback to ensure content 
sufficiency. This produced a dense and semantically rich context index. All queries were 
normalized by converting text to lowercase and stripping punctuation using a simple

28 
 
translation table. No lemmatization or stemming was applied to preserve the original wording, 
particularly since the retrieval methods used—including BM25 and dense embeddings—are 
generally robust to such variations. 
For the purpose of compressing long contexts into manageable retrieval chunks, sentence 
splitting was carried out using the NLTK Punkt tokenizer, with a safe fallback mechanism to 
prevent failure on edge cases. Additionally, to constrain retrieval to relevant domains, a 
filtering mechanism was applied at inference time to restrict candidate spans to those from the 
same document and domain as the query turn, thereby reflecting a realistic enterprise 
deployment scenario where users typically seek information from a bounded context (e.g., 
internal policy documents or product manuals).
