4.2 
Retriever Implementation 
To support retrieval in the RAG pipeline, three types of retrievers were implemented: a sparse 
retriever using BM25, a dense retriever leveraging Sentence-BERT (SBERT) embeddings with 
FAISS indexing, and a hybrid retriever combining the two through Reciprocal Rank Fusion 
(RRF). All retrievers were constructed over the pre-processed Doc2Dial span-level corpus 
derived from the validation split of the dataset, with document restriction applied at evaluation 
time to ensure retrieval occurred only within the same domain and document ID as the gold 
response. 
The sparse retriever was implemented using the rank_bm25.BM25Okapi library in Python. 
Tokenization involved simple lowercasing and punctuation removal, but stopwords were 
retained since BM25 is generally robust to their presence. This setup provided a strong lexical 
matching baseline without requiring external services like Elasticsearch or Pyserini. 
The dense retriever used sentence embeddings generated from pre-trained SBERT models and 
was indexed using FAISS (IndexFlatIP) with cosine similarity on L2-normalized vectors. 
Specifically, multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1 was chosen for its fine-tuning on question-answering 
tasks, offering strong retrieval performance in scenarios requiring semantic understanding. 
This model was used for the dense retriever with Mistral, while the hybrid configuration used 
all-mpnet-base-v2, which offers general-purpose sentence representations with balanced

29 
 
performance. Both encoders produce 768-dimensional vectors with a maximum sequence 
length of 384 tokens. The FAISS index was persisted to disk for reusability, and after indexing, 
query encoding was offloaded to the CPU to free up VRAM during generation. 
The hybrid retriever combined the top-100 results from both BM25 and Dense retrievers using 
Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). RRF assigns scores based on the inverse rank positions of each 
document in the constituent lists. Final fusion weights were set to 𝜔𝑏𝑚25 = 1.0, 𝜔𝑑𝑒𝑛𝑠𝑒= 1.2, 
with a fusion constant C=60, following configurations that balanced the strengths of lexical 
precision and semantic relevance. While an experimental z-score fusion variant was also 
explored, the reported results were derived from RRF due to its stability and interpretability 
across trials. 
This diverse retriever setup allowed for robust comparison of retrieval effectiveness using 
multiple evaluation metrics (e.g., Hit@K, MRR, nDCG@K) and enabled downstream analysis 
of how retrieval strategies affect generation quality in open-domain and domain-specific 
conversational contexts. 
 
Figure 4. Retrieval pipeline showing three retriever configurations. The pre-processed 
span-level corpus from the Doc2Dial dataset feeds into BM25 (sparse), SBERT (dense), and 
Hybrid retrievers. The Hybrid retriever applies Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) on the top-
100 results from BM25 and SBERT to produce a fused ranking. All retrievers output a final 
ranked list of candidate spans used for downstream generation. 
4.3 
Generator Setup  
Once relevant spans were retrieved using the three retriever configurations (BM25 sparse, 
SBERT+FAISS dense, and Hybrid RRF), the next phase involved generating answers using two 
open-source large language models (LLMs): LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2. 
These models were selected due to their strong performance in open-domain question 
answering and their accessibility for academic research. To maintain a fair comparison across 
setups, both models were evaluated on the same retrieved contexts for each query. 
All inference was conducted in Google Colab, using Hugging Face Transformers pipelines and

30 
 
4-bit quantization (NF4) via bitsandbytes, ensuring efficient resource usage without 
sacrificing output quality. The device_map="auto" configuration was used to automatically 
allocate GPU resources. When available, inference was run on A100 GPUs, although fallback 
to T4 or L4 instances did not impact the results, as decoding was fully deterministic. 
Notably, no additional fine-tuning was applied to the models. This design decision ensured a 
consistent evaluation baseline across all retrieval configurations and avoided introducing 
variability due to task-specific adaptation. Instead, grounded generation behavior was 
controlled through prompt engineering and deterministic decoding. 
To control the generation behavior, deterministic decoding settings were applied: 
do_sample=False, temperature=0.0, top_p=1.0, and repetition_penalty ≈ 1.05–1.10. 
These parameters influence how the model samples from its distribution of possible next 
tokens: 
• 
do_sample=False disables random sampling and ensures the model picks the most 
likely output. 
• 
temperature=0.0 removes randomness entirely, always choosing the highest probability 
token. 
• 
top_p=1.0 (nucleus sampling) sets the cumulative probability threshold; a value of 1.0 
means all probable tokens are considered, but combined with do_sample=False, it has no 
effect. 
• 
repetition_penalty discourages repeated phrases or tokens, promoting more fluent and 
varied responses. 
 
 The maximum number of tokens generated per answer was capped at 120 for strict 
configurations and 256 for the general pipeline. These settings ensured consistent and 
comparable generations across different model–retriever combinations. 
To ensure consistent, grounded, and citation-aware outputs, a strict prompting strategy was 
employed. The system prompt instructed the model to: 
• 
Answer only from the numbered context lines. 
• 
Limit responses to two sentences. 
• 
Repeat numbers and dates exactly as written. 
• 
Cite sources using [n] style. 
• 
Avoid echoing the question or headings. 
• 
Use a fallback message if no relevant content is found. 
 
The exact system prompt used during inference is shown in Figure 5. 
 
Figure 5. Strict Prompt Definition Used During Generation 
This prompt was designed to align model outputs closely with the retrieved evidence spans. 
During post-processing, outputs were further sanitized by clamping citation indices to the 
available context, removing echoed headings, and rejecting unsupported numeric values. If

31 
 
necessary, a fallback to extractive snippets was applied to maintain alignment between the 
question and answer. 
Each of the six configurations (three retrievers × two LLMs) was used to generate responses 
for three evaluation settings: 
• 
Automated generation metrics: 300 randomly sampled instances. 
• 
Retrieval metrics: All 4,256 validation queries. 
• 
Human evaluation: 10 curated questions, identical across all configurations. 
 
This systematic pairing of retriever and generator models, under controlled and reproducible 
conditions, formed the core evaluation framework for this thesis. 
Figure 6 illustrates the structured generation pipeline used in this thesis. After retrieving the 
relevant context using one of the three retrieval strategies (BM25, SBERT, or Hybrid), the 
selected passages are embedded into a strict prompt template. This prompt constrains the LLM 
(either LLaMA-2 or Mistral-7B) to produce short, grounded, and citation-aware answers. The 
generated output then undergoes a series of post-processing steps to ensure formatting 
consistency, correct citation usage, and answer validity. This modular and controlled flow 
ensures the generation process remains interpretable and comparable across configurations. 
 
Figure 6. Generation Pipeline Using Retrieved Context and Prompt-Based 
Instructions. 
4.3.1 Runtime Overview  
While training or fine-tuning was not within the scope of this study, we recorded the runtime 
required for retrieval and generation to provide a practical perspective on system efficiency. 
Retrieval evaluation (Hit@K, nDCG, MRR) was relatively fast, taking approximately 5 minutes 
(sparse), 2 minutes (dense), and 6 minutes (hybrid) to process 4,256 queries. Generation time 
for 300 sampled instances varied by model and retrieval strategy. For LLaMA-2-7B, generation 
required approximately 34 minutes (sparse), 22 minutes (dense), and 23 minutes (hybrid). For 
Mistral-7B, the times were 40 minutes, 31 minutes, and 21 minutes respectively. All inference

32 
 
was conducted on Google Colab using A100, T4, or L4 GPUs, depending on availability. These 
durations offer a practical reference point for computational feasibility in real-world RAG 
deployments. 
4.4 
Evaluation Setup  
To evaluate the performance of the RAG configurations, we employed a two-pronged approach 
combining both automated and human assessment protocols. The evaluation was applied 
across six RAG setups, generated by pairing three retrieval strategies—BM25 (sparse), 
SBERT+FAISS (dense), and a Hybrid (RRF fusion of the two)—with two open-source LLMs: 
LLaMA 2 7B Chat and Mistral 7B Instruct v0.2. This setup allowed for a controlled, fair 
comparison across retrieval techniques and model behaviour. 
Automated Evaluation focused on both retrieval and generation quality. For retrieval 
evaluation, we used metrics such as Hit@K (K = 1, 3, 5, 10), Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), 
and normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG@K). These metrics assess how well each 
retriever identifies the ground-truth passage within its top-ranked results. The ground-truth 
was restricted to the same domain and document as the original user query, as defined in the 
Doc2Dial dataset. The evaluation was performed on 4,256 query–answer pairs, one for each 
agent turn that contained annotated span references. 
For generation evaluation, we computed ROUGE-L (F-measure), BERTScore (F1 using 
RoBERTa), and Cosine Similarity (using Sentence-BERT with MiniLM). These metrics 
quantify the similarity between the generated response and the reference answer in terms of 
lexical overlap, semantic alignment, and vector-level similarity, respectively. The scores were 
averaged over 300 randomly sampled instances per RAG configuration, using a fixed seed for 
reproducibility. 
In addition to automated scores, human evaluation was conducted to capture qualitative 
aspects that automated metrics might miss. A set of 10 curated questions, chosen to reflect 
diverse dialogue scenarios, were rated across all configurations. Evaluation was carried out by 
five raters—three domain experts and two end users—who scored each answer based on four 
key criteria: Factuality, Relevance, Clarity, and Completeness. The rubric was based on insights 
from semi-structured interviews and included a Likert scale from 1 (very poor) to 5 (excellent). 
Each response was compared against a ground-truth answer to anchor the ratings and improve 
consistency. 
By combining quantitative and qualitative assessments, this evaluation setup offers a 
comprehensive and balanced view of system performance, identifying not only how often 
relevant content is retrieved or generated but also how well it meets human expectations in 
enterprise support scenarios. 
4.5 
Result Management and Output Artifacts 
To maintain transparency and reproducibility throughout the experimental workflow, all 
evaluation outputs were systematically recorded, organized, and stored using standardized 
formats. The structure allowed for efficient analysis and traceability of both intermediate and 
final outputs across the six RAG configurations. 
For retrieval evaluation, the ranked results were saved in structured JSON files (e.g., 
sparse_rag_bm25_results_.json), capturing document IDs, scores, and ranks for each query.

33 
 
The generation outputs, along with their associated contexts, reference answers, and 
automated metric scores (e.g., ROUGE-L, BERTScore, cosine similarity), were saved in CSV 
files (e.g., gen_records_sparse.csv). 
The human evaluation scores were collected manually using structured Excel sheets, where 
five evaluators rated ten responses from each configuration across four criteria: factuality, 
relevance, clarity, and completeness. These sheets included individual and average scores, 
serving as the basis for manual aggregation and further comparison with automated metrics. 
For visual analysis, several types of plots and charts were generated to illustrate differences 
between retriever-generator combinations. These included: 
• 
Scatter plots showing pairwise metric comparisons across configurations. 
• 
Heatmaps comparing average scores. 
• 
Bar charts for ranking configurations based on selected metrics. 
• 
Line plots showing metric trends across rank thresholds (e.g., Hit@K, nDCG@K). 
 
All visualizations were saved in PNG format for inclusion in the thesis and future 
presentations. The artifacts were timestamped and version-controlled, with backups 
maintained for reliability and consistency. 
This structured result management approach contributes to the reproducibility, transparency, 
and robustness of the study, ensuring that outputs can be reviewed, validated, or reused in 
future work. 
5 
Results 
This chapter presents the results obtained from evaluating six Retrieval-Augmented 
Generation (RAG) configurations. These configurations combine three types of retrievers—
sparse, dense, and hybrid—with two different language models, Llama 2 and Mistral 7B. The 
evaluation was conducted using both automated metrics and human assessments in order to 
provide a comprehensive view of each configuration’s performance. 
The automated evaluation includes retrieval effectiveness metrics such as Hit@k, Mean 
Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG) at various 
cutoff values. For the generation component, the quality of responses was assessed using 
ROUGE-L, BERTScore, and sentence-level cosine similarity. These metrics quantify 
performance in a consistent and objective manner. 
In addition to automated metrics, human evaluation was conducted to capture qualitative 
aspects of model responses that automated scores may overlook. Expert raters assessed a 
curated set of generated answers based on four criteria: factual correctness, relevance, clarity, 
and completeness. Ratings were provided on a five-point Likert scale, and results were 
aggregated to reveal strengths and weaknesses across different model-retriever pairings. 
The structure of this chapter is as follows. Section 5.1 presents the results from automated 
evaluation, with subsections highlighting key findings, metric interpretations, and method 
comparisons. Section 5.2 focuses on human evaluation results and includes observations on 
response quality, illustrations of specific limitations, the importance of human judgment in 
assessing contextual responses, and analysis of correlations between human and automated 
scores.

34 
Together, these results provide both statistical insights and practical reflections on the 
effectiveness of different RAG setups in the context of enterprise-level information retrieval 
and generation. 
5.1 
Automated Evaluation Results 
The automated evaluation focused on measuring the performance of each retrieval and 
generation configuration across a set of quantitative metrics. This evaluation helps objectively 
compare how well each RAG setup retrieves relevant context and generates high-quality 
answers. The evaluation was carried out on 300 generation samples and 4,256 retrieval queries 
using standard metrics. 
5.1.1 Key Findings 
Since the retrieval module was identical across both LLaMA-2 and Mistral setups, retrieval 
results were the same for both models. Therefore, retrieval plots are reported only once (using 
LLaMA-2 as reference) to avoid redundancy. The retrieval effectiveness was evaluated using 
Hit@k (k = 1, 3, 5, 10), Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and normalized Discounted Cumulative 
Gain (nDCG@k). As shown in Figure 7, the dense retriever based on Sentence-BERT 
consistently outperformed the sparse BM25 and hybrid RRF approaches across all Hit@k 
values. This trend was consistent across both language models (Llama 2 and Mistral 7B). 
Figure 7: Hit@k Comparison for Llama-2-7B / Mistral-7B 
Importantly, retrieval results are shared across both LLaMA 2 and Mistral 7B, since the 
retrieval stage in RAG is model-agnostic—meaning that all retrieval evaluations were 
conducted independently of the generator used later. The retrieved documents for a given 
query remain the same regardless of whether LLaMA or Mistral was used in the subsequent 
generation step. As such, Figures 7 to 10 only display retrieval performance for LLaMA 2, but 
these results apply equally to Mistral 7B. 
To avoid redundancy and maintain visual clarity, plots from Mistral 7B were not included in

In terms of generation quality, Figures 11 and 12 present the results across ROUGE-L, 
BERTScore F1, and SBERT cosine similarity for LLaMA-2-7B and Mistral-7B, respectively. At 
first glance, the values appear very similar across the different retrieval strategies, which makes 
it difficult to determine whether the small variations are meaningful. To address this, the 
results are reported as mean values with standard deviation error bars, and statistical 
significance testing was applied to verify whether the observed differences are genuine or 
simply due to variance in the samples.

37 
 
 
Figure 11. Generation metrics for LLaMA-2-7B across retrieval strategies. Bars show 
mean ± standard deviation; ANOVA found no significant differences (p > 0.05). 
 
 
Figure 12. Generation metrics for Mistral-7B across retrieval strategies. Bars show mean 
± standard deviation; ANOVA found no significant differences (p > 0.05)

38 
 
Across both models, ROUGE-L and BERTScore F1 values were stable across retrieval 
strategies, with only marginal differences in the mean scores (ROUGE-L ≈ 0.171–0.179; 
BERTScore F1 ≈ 0.849–0.851). Cosine similarity showed slightly more variation, with hybrid 
retrieval achieving the highest mean values (0.412 ± 0.245 for LLaMA-2-7B and 0.413 ± 0.245 
for Mistral-7B), suggesting improved semantic alignment of generated responses when both 
sparse and dense signals were fused. 
To test whether these differences were statistically significant, one-way ANOVA was conducted 
across the three retrieval strategies for each metric. For LLaMA-2-7B, results showed no 
significant differences across retrievers for ROUGE-L (F=0.184, p=0.8324), BERTScore F1 
(F=0.215, p=0.8067), or cosine similarity (F=2.473, p=0.0849). Similarly, for Mistral-7B, the 
differences were not significant for ROUGE-L (F=0.181, p=0.8347), BERTScore F1 (F=0.128, 
p=0.8796), or cosine similarity (F=2.496, p=0.0830). All p-values exceeded the 0.05 
threshold, indicating that while hybrid retrieval produced marginally higher cosine similarity 
means, the differences are not statistically significant. 
These findings confirm that the apparent similarity in Figures 11 and 12 is not coincidental: the 
generation quality across sparse, dense, and hybrid retrieval strategies remains statistically 
indistinguishable. Nevertheless, small numerical trends are noteworthy. Hybrid retrieval 
consistently yielded the highest cosine similarity means (≈0.412–0.413), suggesting it may 
provide slightly richer semantic grounding for generation. Dense retrieval, while strongest in 
ranking performance, did not translate this advantage into significantly higher generation 
scores. Sparse retrieval remained the weakest across all metrics. Taken together, the evidence 
indicates that although no retrieval strategy produced a statistically significant improvement 
in generation quality, hybrid retrieval showed the most favourable tendencies, particularly in 
semantic similarity. 
Lastly, Figures 13 and 14 plot the relationship between retrieval ranking (nDCG@10) and 
generation clarity (ROUGE-L). Interestingly, both show a slightly negative correlation, where 
setups with stronger ranking (like dense retrieval) resulted in slightly lower ROUGE scores 
compared to hybrid retrieval. This inverse trend suggests that improving strict ranking may 
not always translate to better generated output and that diversity in retrieved content could 
benefit generation. 
 
Figure 13: Correlation Between nDCG@10 and ROUGE-L for Llama-2-7B

39 
 
 
Figure 14: Correlation Between nDCG@10 and ROUGE-L for Mistral-7B 
 
These results are in line with previous research findings. For example, in the BSharedRAG 
study by Chen et al. (2024), ROUGE-L scores across various retriever–generator 
configurations ranged between 8.12 and 15.92, and BERTScore F1 values ranged from 
approximately 62.5 to 77.98, depending on the model architecture and setup. In comparison, 
our observed BERTScore F1 (≈0.849–0.851) and ROUGE-L (≈0.171–0.179) are similarly 
stable across retrieval strategies, though higher in absolute terms, likely due to dataset and 
prompt differences. This consistency reinforces the validity of our evaluation and places our 
findings within the expected performance band for domain-specific RAG systems. 
5.1.2 Interpretation 
The automated evaluation results reveal several insightful patterns regarding the retrieval and 
generation configurations. Firstly, the dense retriever (SBERT-based) consistently yielded 
superior retrieval metrics across all evaluated criteria including Hit@k, MRR, and nDCG@k 
indicating its strong semantic matching capabilities compared to traditional sparse BM25 or 
hybrid approaches. This confirms the effectiveness of semantic dense retrieval in domain-
specific conversational settings where lexical overlap alone (as in BM25) may not suffice. 
Interestingly, the hybrid retrieval method underperformed in most ranking metrics despite 
being a fusion of the top-100 results from BM25 and SBERT using Reciprocal Rank Fusion 
(RRF). This suggests that, in this use case, combining sparse and dense rankings may introduce 
conflicting signals or noise, diluting the quality of the top-ranked contexts. The 
underperformance of hybrid retrieval was consistent across both LLMs, indicating it was not 
model-specific but rather tied to the retrieval strategy itself. 
However, the generation metrics reveal a more nuanced picture. While dense retrieval excelled 
at ranking, hybrid retrieval achieved marginally better performance in cosine similarity — a 
metric reflecting semantic closeness between generated and reference answers. This pattern 
suggests that although dense retrievers return the most topically relevant passages, hybrid 
retrieval may introduce broader or complementary context that helps LLMs generate answers 
that are semantically closer to the gold responses.

40 
 
Importantly, ANOVA tests confirmed that none of the observed differences across retrieval 
strategies were statistically significant (p > 0.05 for all metrics). This indicates that in practice, 
the choice of retriever does not guarantee superior generation quality. Still, the trend toward 
higher cosine similarity with hybrid retrieval provides a valuable insight: for enterprise help-
desk scenarios, retrieval diversity may sometimes support generation as much as, or more 
than, strict ranking precision. 
Overall, the automated evaluation shows that while dense retrieval is clearly the best option 
for retrieval in isolation, the interaction with generation is more nuanced. Hybrid retrieval 
shows some potential to support generation quality, though without statistically significant 
gains in this study. This highlights that generation benefits not only from precision but also 
from diversity in retrieved documents, an insight that warrants further study or even custom 
retriever designs tailored for LLM prompting. Notably, these findings align with Chen et al. 
(2024), who also observed stable ROUGE-L and BERTScore values across RAG variants, 
suggesting that generation quality differences are often marginal even when retrieval strategies 
vary. 
To complement these automated findings, the next section presents results from the human 
evaluation, providing an additional perspective on how retrieval strategies affect the quality of 
generated answers. 
5.2 
Human Evaluation Results 
To complement automated metrics and capture nuanced aspects of response quality, human 
evaluation was conducted on a curated subset of 10 representative queries. Three domain 
experts assessed the outputs generated by each model configuration using a structured 
evaluation form. Each response was scored across four key criteria: Factual Correctness, 
Relevance, Clarity, and Completeness, using a 5-point Likert scale. The evaluation was 
performed using an Excel-based template, which enabled structured, side-by-side comparison 
of responses across retrieval strategies and generators. A reference to this evaluation template 
is provided in the appendix C. Beyond its role in this study, the evaluation template also 
constitutes a methodological outcome. It provides a structured and reusable framework for 
scoring RAG outputs that future studies in enterprise or domain-specific contexts may adopt 
or adapt as a reference for systematic human evaluation. 
This form of manual assessment provides an essential layer of insight into how well the 
generated outputs align with human expectations and domain-specific information needs. 
While automated metrics are useful for benchmarking, they often fail to capture semantic 
correctness or contextual adequacy — aspects that human raters can evaluate more reliably. 
The aggregated scores and heatmaps (Figures 15, 16 and table 1) summarize the overall trends 
across all six RAG configurations evaluated in the study. 
The evaluation process required on average 60 minutes per participant, with durations ranging 
from 50 minutes (fastest) to 70 minutes (slowest) for completing all 10 curated questions. 
Domain experts generally completed the task more quickly than end-users, likely reflecting 
their greater familiarity with the enterprise documentation and support workflows. While 
these differences in completion time suggest an influence of domain expertise on evaluation 
speed, the resulting ratings were consistent across both groups, indicating that length of 
professional experience did not bias the evaluation outcomes.

41 
 
5.2.1 Key Findings 
The human evaluation results, as visualized in Figure 16 and Figure 17, reveal clear differences 
in response quality across the evaluated RAG configurations. Among the six methods, Dense 
retrieval with LLaMA-2-7B achieved the highest average scores across all four evaluation 
dimensions — factual correctness, relevance, clarity, and completeness — indicating its strong 
overall performance in generating accurate and contextually appropriate responses. This is 
followed closely by the hybrid retrieval approach (RRF), also paired with LLaMA-2-7B, which 
exhibited slightly lower completeness and factual scores but remained competitive. 
 
Figure 15 — Human evaluation heatmap for LLaMA-2-7B across three retrieval methods 
(BM25, Dense, Hybrid), showing average scores for four criteria. 
 
For Mistral-7B, a similar trend emerged: the dense retrieval method again achieved the highest 
scores across most categories, suggesting that dense semantic representations contribute 
significantly to producing factually correct and fluent answers. Conversely, sparse retrieval 
(BM25) methods tended to perform worse across both models, particularly in completeness 
and factuality. 
 
Figure 16 — Human evaluation heatmap for Mistral-7B across three retrieval methods 
(BM25, Dense, Hybrid), showing average scores for four criteria.

Overall, the findings underscore the importance of retrieval quality in RAG pipelines. While 
both LLMs showed strong capabilities, the variation in response quality was more closely tied 
to the retrieval method than the generator model itself.

5.2.2 Interpretation 
The human evaluation results provide valuable insight into how retrieval strategies and LLM 
configurations influence qualitative aspects of generated responses. As shown in Table 1 
(Aggregated Human Evaluation Scores by Model and Evaluation Dimension), configurations 
using dense retrieval consistently outperformed other retrieval strategies across all 
dimensions—factuality, relevance, clarity, and completeness—for both LLaMA 2 and Mistral. 
This affirms that dense retrieval better captures semantic similarity, allowing models to ground 
their responses in more contextually appropriate spans. 
Interestingly, hybrid retrieval despite its theoretical advantage in combining lexical and 
semantic signals via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) did not consistently outperform the pure 
dense retriever. This is further evident in Figure 16 and Figure 17 (Heatmaps for LLaMA 2 and 
Mistral), where hybrid configurations show moderate scores, occasionally trailing behind 
dense. This suggests that hybrid fusion, while robust in theory, may introduce retrieval noise 
or diminish precision if not optimally weighted. 
The BM25-only setup scored the lowest across most evaluation dimensions, emphasizing the 
limitations of sparse retrieval in capturing semantic context in multi-turn, span-grounded 
dialogues like Doc2Dial. The scores corroborate the retrieval metrics discussed earlier and 
reinforce that retrieval quality has a direct and measurable impact on the human-perceived 
quality of generated outputs. 
On the generator side, Mistral-7B-Instruct marginally outperformed LLaMA-2-7B-Chat, 
especially in clarity and completeness, as seen in both the aggregated scores and heatmap 
visualizations. This reflects Mistral’s tendency to produce more coherent and well-rounded 
answers, even with the same retrieved content. However, the difference in generation quality 
was less stark than the variation caused by retrieval strategies, suggesting that retrieval 
configuration is the more dominant factor in response quality.The remainder of this section 
(and Section 6.2) discusses why this retrieval→generation bottleneck arises and how it might 
be tested and mitigated. 
A plausible explanation for this pattern is that the retriever effectively defines the evidence 
space available to the generator: if the retriever fails to surface relevant, explanatory spans, the 
generator cannot produce a grounded, complete answer regardless of its own capabilities. 
Conversely, when retrieved contexts contain clear, explanatory passages, even relatively small 
instruction-tuned models can produce acceptable outputs. In addition, both generators used 
here are similar in scale (≈7B) and were evaluated with deterministic decoding and the same 
grounding prompt, which reduces generator-induced variance; consequently, differences 
introduced by retrieval are relatively more visible.  
Overall, these findings confirm the importance of retrieval in RAG pipelines and align with 
expectations voiced during the interview phase, where evaluators emphasized the need for 
relevant, complete, and well-grounded answers. The visual heatmaps (Figures 15 and 16) 
reinforce these points, providing a clear view of how each configuration performed across 
evaluative criteria and model pairings. 
One notable observation is the discrepancy between quantitative metrics and human 
evaluations for the Sparse+LLaMA2 setup. While this configuration scored lower on average 
across automated metrics like ROUGE-L and BERTScore compared to dense and hybrid 
retrieval setups, it still performed reasonably well in human evaluation. For instance, in 
response to the question "What happens if I don't get a payment within 15 days?", the

45 
 
Sparse+LLaMA2 model generated the answer "If you don't get a payment within 15 days, 
please contact your regional office or the Veterans Help Line at 800-827-1000." Human 
raters judged this response as factually correct, clear, and complete (Factual Correctness: 4.5, 
Relevance: 4.5, Clarity: 4.0, Completeness: 4.0). This suggests that, despite lower token-level 
overlap typical of sparse retrievers, such outputs can still meet user expectations and perform 
well in real-world contexts. This disconnect illustrates the limitations of automated metrics 
and highlights the importance of human-centered evaluation in enterprise help-desk 
applications. 
5.2.3 Illustrative Failure Case: Dense Retrieval with LLaMA-2 
Despite the generally strong performance of the dense retriever across both LLaMA-2 and 
Mistral models, isolated failure cases reveal important limitations that automated metrics 
alone cannot surface. One illustrative example is shown in Figure 17, where the response from 
the LLaMA-2-7B with dense (SBERT) retrieval to the question “What are the benefits for 
severely injured active duty members?” was significantly deficient in all human evaluation 
dimensions. In this case, the model responded: 
“You don’t need to wait for a rating. [1]” 
 
Figure 17: Example of a Low-Scoring Response from LLaMA-2 with Dense (SBERT) 
Retrieval, Rated Poor in Factuality, Relevance, Clarity, and Completeness 
This response received the lowest possible score (1 out of 5) across factual correctness, 
relevance, clarity, and completeness, indicating a complete breakdown in the generation 
pipeline. The reply lacked context, omitted the necessary background on VR&E benefits, and 
did not explain the policy referenced in the source document. It also failed to offer any helpful 
information or direction to the user. 
Notably, this failure occurred despite the dense retriever typically yielding the most 
semantically aligned passages. This discrepancy suggests that strong retrieval performance 
does not guarantee coherent or complete generation, especially when the prompt context is 
misinterpreted, or the relevant portion of the document is underutilized by the LLM. 
Additionally, LLMs may generate confidently incorrect or vague answers if the retrieved 
context is ambiguous or too short. 
This example underscores the importance of human evaluation as a complement to automated 
metrics. While dense retrieval may excel in Hit@k or nDCG, it still requires rigorous inspection 
through human lenses to validate the practical usefulness and trustworthiness of generated 
answers in real-world enterprise applications. 
This example underscores the importance of human evaluation as a complement to automated 
metrics. While dense retrieval may excel in metrics such as Hit@K or nDCG, it still requires

46 
 
rigorous inspection through human lenses to validate the practical usefulness and 
trustworthiness of generated answers in real-world enterprise applications. More broadly, this 
case reflects an open research gap: bridging retrieval quality with generation quality remains 
a non-trivial challenge. Even when retrieval surfaces the “right” passages, the model must still 
ground its responses effectively. 
Recent research suggests several approaches to mitigate this gap. These include prompt 
engineering strategies that condition the model to rely more explicitly on retrieved evidence 
(Komeili, Shuster, & Weston, 2022), re-ranking or answer-aware retrieval techniques that 
emphasize explanatory rather than merely semantically similar passages (Izacard & Grave, 
2021), and alignment methods such as reinforcement learning with human feedback 
(Christiano et al., 2017; Bai et al., 2022). However, no single approach fully resolves the issue, 
making it an active area of research in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). This thesis 
therefore positions such failures not as isolated flaws but as fundamental limitations of current 
RAG pipelines that future work must address. 
5.2.4 Correlation Between Automated Metrics and Human 
Judgments 
To understand the relationship between human evaluation criteria and commonly used 
automated metrics, we computed the Pearson correlation coefficient across all six RAG 
configurations. This analysis considered three automated generation metrics—ROUGE-L_F, 
BERTScore_F1, and Cosine Similarity—and four human judgment criteria: Factual 
Correctness, Relevance, Clarity, and Completeness. 
The results, presented in Figure 18, indicate weak to no correlation between automated metrics 
and human ratings. Among the automated metrics, ROUGE-L_F showed the strongest positive 
correlations, particularly with Completeness (r = 0.22) and Relevance (r = 0.21), although even 
these values suggest only a weak linear relationship. BERTScore_F1 and Cosine Similarity, 
often regarded as semantic similarity metrics, failed to capture human-assessed quality 
accurately, with some correlations being negative or near zero (e.g., BERTScore vs. Factual 
Correctness: r = -0.13). 
 
Figure 18. Pearson Correlation Heatmap Between Automated Metrics and Human 
Evaluation Scores Across RAG Configurations.

47 
 
These findings underscore a critical limitation of relying solely on automated metrics to 
evaluate conversational responses, particularly in domain-specific or enterprise contexts 
where accuracy, completeness, and clarity are essential. While automated metrics provide 
efficiency and consistency, they appear insufficient to fully reflect nuanced human judgments, 
especially regarding factual correctness and response coherence. 
The Pearson correlation coefficient was chosen for this analysis as it measures the linear 
association between two continuous variables. It provides insight into how well one metric 
predicts another and is widely used in behavioural and computational research for this purpose 
(Schober, Boer, & Schwarte, 2018). 
Taken together, these results reinforce the importance of human-in-the-loop evaluation for 
reliable assessment of language model outputs. Automated metrics should be interpreted with 
caution and complemented by qualitative or human-centric methods when high-stakes 
accuracy is required. 
5.3 
Analysis of the joint results 
By synthesizing the outcomes from both automated metrics and human evaluations, a more 
nuanced understanding emerges of how each RAG configuration performs across dimensions 
that matter in practical enterprise settings. While automated metrics like ROUGE-L_f and 
BERTScore provide objective, scalable measures of lexical and semantic similarity, they do not 
fully capture user-centered qualities such as factual accuracy, contextual completeness, and 
clarity. This gap was especially evident in the weak or negative correlations observed between 
BERTScore/Cosine Similarity and human ratings, in contrast to the moderate positive 
correlation shown by ROUGE-L_f. 
From both evaluation modalities, the Dense+Mistral configuration consistently outperformed 
other combinations. It achieved the highest scores across nearly all retrieval and generation 
metrics (e.g., Hit@K, nDCG@K, MRR, ROUGE-L_f), and also received top human scores for 
factual correctness (3.9), clarity (3.9), relevance (3.9), and completeness (3.4). This suggests 
that Mistral’s generation style, when paired with semantically rich dense retrieval, delivers 
responses that are not only relevant in content but also coherent and grounded in the user’s 
context. 
The Hybrid retriever configurations also showed competitive generation quality, especially in 
terms of completeness and factuality, but did not consistently outperform dense retrieval. This 
highlights that while hybrid strategies can mitigate sparse and dense retrieval limitations via 
Reciprocal Rank Fusion, they may also introduce noise or redundancy that affects clarity and 
focus. 
Interestingly, Sparse+Llama2, while scoring lower on automated metrics, showed a reasonable 
performance in some human-rated aspects such as clarity. This reinforces the notion that even 
simpler retrieval strategies, when coupled with aligned prompting, can generate acceptably 
useful answers for end users. 
The joint analysis also reveals a key trade-off in RAG systems: configurations optimized for 
lexical matching (like sparse retrievers) may retrieve less diverse content but offer more direct 
matches for certain queries, while dense retrievers improve semantic coverage but can 
introduce irrelevant or overly verbose content. Human evaluation captures these subtleties, 
validating the importance of complementing automated assessments with qualitative 
judgment, especially when developing enterprise-grade conversational systems.

48 
 
In summary, the convergence of both quantitative and qualitative assessments strengthens the 
reliability of the findings. Dense retrieval paired with Mistral-7B emerged as the most balanced 
configuration in terms of both algorithmic performance and user-centered quality, offering 
valuable guidance for future deployments of domain-specific RAG systems. 
6 
Discussion 
This chapter reflects critically on the results presented in Chapter 5 and connects them to prior 
research, methodological choices, and broader ethical and societal implications. The aim is to 
contextualize the findings and explore their significance, both in terms of academic 
understanding and practical relevance for enterprise-level conversational AI systems. The 
discussion is structured into three subsections. First, it compares the findings with previous 
studies and theoretical frameworks (Section 6.1). Then, it assesses the impact of the chosen 
methods and implementation on the outcomes (Section 6.2). Finally, it considers the ethical 
and societal aspects that emerge from the deployment of retrieval-augmented generation 
models in real-world support scenarios (Section 6.3). 
6.1 
Previous Research 
The findings of this study both align with and expand upon prior research on Retrieval-
Augmented Generation (RAG) and response quality evaluation in domain-specific dialogue 
systems. Earlier studies have highlighted the value of dense retrieval methods over sparse 
approaches for open-domain question answering tasks due to their stronger semantic 
matching capabilities (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Izacard & Grave, 2021). This was confirmed in 
our study, where dense retrieval using SBERT consistently outperformed BM25 in terms of 
Hit@K and nDCG@K metrics. 
Additionally, the hybrid retrieval approach, which leverages Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), 
performed competitively and showed improved robustness in generation metrics. This 
supports prior findings that hybrid methods can mitigate the weaknesses of both sparse and 
dense retrievers and often perform well across diverse queries (Luan et al., 2021). 
When comparing generation quality, our results support the observation by Zhang et al. (2019) 
and Feng et al. (2021) that automatic metrics such as ROUGE-L and BERTScore provide 
partial indicators of human-perceived quality but fail to capture completeness and factual 
correctness in more complex, grounded tasks. This was evident in the weak or inconsistent 
correlations observed between BERTScore or Cosine Similarity and human judgments across 
all configurations. 
The stronger correlation between ROUGE-L and human ratings—especially for relevance and 
completeness—highlights that even simple overlap-based metrics can be informative in 
structured, context-limited environments like Doc2Dial. Similar patterns were observed in 
studies by Mousavi et al. (2022), which emphasized the necessity of multi-dimensional human 
evaluation when assessing response generation quality. 
Furthermore, the comparative performance of Mistral-7B and LLaMA-2-7B reflects emerging 
trends in open-source LLM design. Notably, Mistral-7B consistently exceeds LLaMA-2 13B 
across automated and human-evaluated benchmarks (Jiang et al., 2023). Our human 
evaluation also found a slight edge for Mistral-7B in both clarity and completeness, suggesting 
that its architectural enhancements and instruction-tuned variant may better support high-

49 
 
quality generative performance. However, both models struggled with factual grounding, 
reaffirming the difficulty of controlling hallucinations in generative systems without retrieval-
aware fine-tuning (Shuster et al., 2021). 
Overall, this study corroborates existing insights in the literature while contributing a 
systematic evaluation of retrieval-generation configurations under real-world enterprise 
constraints. Our use of Doc2Dial and end-user evaluations adds empirical grounding to claims 
around hybrid RAG design, evaluation reliability, and model limitations. 
6.2 
Methods, implementation and results 
The design of this study was structured to ensure a fair and comprehensive evaluation of 
different retrieval strategies within Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. Several 
methodological and implementation choices significantly influenced the results. 
First, the use of the Doc2Dial dataset for evaluation ensured a realistic and domain-grounded 
testbed. This dataset, which consists of multi-turn dialogues grounded in structured 
documents, provided a robust context for evaluating both retrieval and generation capabilities. 
However, the decision to evaluate only on the validation split—without model fine-tuning—
means the results reflect out-of-the-box model capabilities, not their potential after domain 
adaptation. 
In the retrieval phase, three strategies—BM25 (sparse), SBERT+FAISS (dense), and Hybrid 
(RRF fusion)—were systematically compared. The sparse and dense retrieval strategies used 
consistent preprocessing pipelines and fixed hyperparameters across both LLMs, ensuring fair 
comparability. While BM25 is a proven baseline, the inclusion of a hybrid approach using 
Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) allowed us to explore synergies between lexical and semantic 
search. The RRF weighting choices (𝑤_bm25 = 1.0, 𝑤_dense = 1.2, 𝐶 = 60) were fixed after 
preliminary experiments and influenced final ranking behaviour—highlighting how even small 
tuning decisions can impact downstream generation. 
For the generation phase, two open-weight large language models—Mistral 7B Instruct and 
LLaMA 2 7B Chat—were used in combination with the retrieval strategies. Their use of strict 
prompting, quantized inference, and deterministic decoding ensured consistent output quality 
and replicability. However, since both models were run in zero-shot mode, without task-
specific fine-tuning, some hallucination and citation errors were observed in complex cases. 
These were captured both quantitatively and in qualitative error analysis. 
A key implementation strength was the dual evaluation strategy: automated metrics provided 
scalable, objective assessments, while human evaluation offered qualitative insight into factual 
correctness, clarity, relevance, completeness, and citation faithfulness. This hybrid approach 
enabled nuanced findings that would be missed using metrics alone. 
For example, while Sparse+LLaMA2 scored lower on automated metrics like cosine similarity 
and BERTScore, human raters gave it reasonable scores in terms of factual accuracy and 
completeness. In one evaluated case (see Human Evaluation Sample #4), although the 
generated response was relatively short and ranked poorly by cosine similarity, the human 
evaluators agreed that the core information was accurate, grounded in the context, and 
complete for the query asked. This suggests that automated metrics can sometimes undervalue 
concise yet sufficient answers, particularly when they rely heavily on surface-form similarity. 
Such gaps underscore the importance of combining human and automated evaluation.

50 
 
Additionally, while Figure 11 and Figure 12 showed visually similar generation scores across 
configurations, statistical testing confirmed this impression: ANOVA tests found no 
statistically significant differences in ROUGE-L, BERTScore F1, or cosine similarity across 
retrieval methods (all p > 0.05). For instance, cosine similarity scores for hybrid retrieval were 
marginally higher, but the difference was not significant. These findings reinforce that 
numerical variations alone do not imply meaningful differences in output quality, unless tested 
through appropriate significance tests. 
Still, there are limitations. The human evaluation set was small (10 curated questions), which 
constrains the ability to generalize findings statistically. While inter-rater reliability was not 
formally calculated due to privacy considerations (see Section 2.1.6.2), mitigation strategies 
were applied through a standardized rubric and aggregation of scores across five evaluators. 
Additionally, the retrieval metrics remained constant across models due to shared indices, 
which limited the ability to observe how different generation models might compensate for 
retrieval differences. 
Overall, the chosen methods and pipeline offered a transparent, modular, and reproducible 
framework, well-suited for comparative studies of RAG systems. While some implementation 
choices (e.g., number of samples, prompt strictness, lack of fine-tuning) were constrained by 
time and computational resources, they still enabled meaningful insights into how different 
configurations affect both retrieval and generative performance. These findings are directly 
relevant to enterprise applications of RAG systems, where interpretability, citation control, and 
groundedness are critical. 
6.2.1 Why retrieval often dominates generation — speculation and 
implications 
The empirical pattern observed in this work — that variation in response quality is more 
strongly associated with the retrieval method than with the generator model — is consistent 
with a conceptual “evidence ceiling” in RAG pipelines: retrieval determines the set of passages 
the generator may condition on, and therefore establishes an upper bound on the factual 
completeness and specificity the generator can achieve (Lewis et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 
2020). Several, non-mutually exclusive explanations account for why retrieval effects appear 
larger than generator effects in our experiments: 
1. Evidence availability: If the retriever fails to include the crucial explanatory span(s), the 
generator lacks the information necessary to answer correctly, so even a stronger generator 
cannot recover missing facts. 
2. Signal-to-noise and span quality: High ranking scores (e.g., nDCG) do not guarantee 
that retrieved spans are explanatory or phrased in a way the generator can use; terse or 
context-fragment spans can be semantically relevant but insufficient to support a complete 
answer. 
3. Prompt and consumption mismatch: Generators must use retrieved content 
effectively; suboptimal prompt framing or truncation of context limits how much the LLM 
can leverage retrieved evidence (Izacard & Grave, 2021). 
4. Model parity and deterministic decoding: Both LLaMA-2 and Mistral in this study 
are instruction-tuned 7B models and were evaluated with deterministic decoding. The 
smaller variability between these generators reduces the observable generator effect 
compared to retrieval differences.

51 
 
5. Retrieval diversity vs. precision trade-off: Methods that maximize strict relevance 
(e.g., dense ranking) may reduce context diversity; in some tasks broader complementary 
context (as hybrid retrieval can provide) helps produce more complete answers. 
These mechanisms suggest that retrieval is often the dominant lever in the short pipeline used 
here. That does not imply the generator is unimportant — rather, it implies an interaction: 
retrieval sets the upper bound on correctness, while generator design (prompting, model scale, 
fine-tuning, or alignment) determines how well that available evidence is translated into a 
coherent, complete output. 
6.2.2 Bridging the Retrieval–Generation Gap: Implications, 
Analyses, and Future Directions 
The finding that retrieval methods often exert a stronger influence on response quality than 
the choice of generator has several important implications for both research and practice. From 
a practical perspective, it suggests that improving retrieval design is often the most effective 
way to raise overall system quality. Several approaches may help narrow the gap between 
strong retrieval and coherent generation. Retrieval objectives can be adapted to prioritize 
explanatory spans rather than merely semantically similar passages, thereby ensuring that the 
generator receives content that is not only relevant but also sufficiently detailed (Izacard & 
Grave, 2021). Retrieval diversity can also be increased, for example by combining lexical and 
dense methods or by applying re-ranking strategies that highlight complementary evidence. 
On the generation side, carefully designed prompts can condition the model to explicitly cite 
and reason over retrieved content, encouraging it to ground its answers in evidence (Komeili, 
Shuster, & Weston, 2022). Another useful diagnostic is to provide the model with gold-
standard passages directly—sometimes called an oracle retrieval setup—in order to measure 
the upper bound of what the generator could achieve if retrieval were perfect. Finally, generator 
alignment through fine-tuning or reinforcement learning with human feedback offers another 
pathway to strengthen the model’s ability to use retrieved information faithfully (Christiano et 
al., 2017; Bai et al., 2022). While promising, these strategies are unlikely to be sufficient in 
isolation, and combining retrieval- and generation-oriented techniques will likely be necessary 
for robust improvements. 
Moving from speculation to evidence requires more systematic analyses. Future studies should 
compare different retrievers while holding the generator constant, and conversely test different 
generators with the same retrieval pipeline, to quantify the relative contribution of each 
component. Varying the number and type of retrieved passages—for example, comparing top-
1 versus top-10 passages, or contrasting lexical and dense retrieval—would clarify how evidence 
breadth and quality affect generation. Providing gold-standard passages to the generator can 
also serve as a benchmark for evaluating the true potential of a model when retrieval is not a 
limiting factor. Moreover, correlating retrieval metrics such as Hit@k or nDCG with human 
judgments would shed light on which retrieval properties—precision, diversity, or coverage of 
explanatory spans—best predict actual response usefulness. These analyses would provide 
stronger empirical grounding for the observation that retrieval is often the dominant factor in 
RAG pipelines. 
For practitioners, the key message is that retrieval design should often be prioritized, since it 
defines the evidence space available to the model and thereby sets an upper bound on factual 
accuracy and completeness. Generator alignment through prompting, fine-tuning, or RLHF 
remains important, but its impact is constrained by the quality of retrieved evidence. The

52 
 
conclusions of this thesis are also conditional: they reflect experiments with two instruction-
tuned 7B models, deterministic decoding, and a limited sample of human evaluations. Future 
work with larger generators, more sophisticated prompting strategies, or task-specific 
alignment may alter the balance of influence between retrieval and generation. 
6.3 
Ethical and societal aspects  
The ethical and societal implications of deploying Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) 
systems, particularly in enterprise contexts, are complex and demand careful attention across 
the stages of data handling, system development, deployment, and evaluation. 
Data and Privacy Considerations 
This study utilized the Doc2Dial dataset (Feng et al., 2021), a publicly available and 
anonymized benchmark designed for research in document-grounded dialogue systems. Since 
the dataset does not contain any personally identifiable information and was explicitly curated 
for academic use, no personal data processing was involved in this thesis work. However, the 
broader applicability of such models to enterprise settings may involve sensitive or proprietary 
documentation. In real-world implementations, organizations must ensure that data 
governance practices align with privacy regulations such as the General Data Protection 
Regulation (GDPR) in the EU. This includes principles of transparency, user consent, data 
minimization, and the right to explanation. 
Transparency and Explainability 
One of the design goals of this thesis was to build RAG configurations that enable traceable 
and grounded responses. The use of numbered citations (e.g., “[3]”) in generated outputs was 
introduced to enhance explainability and support user trust. While this structure improves 
transparency, evaluation results showed that hallucination and citation errors still occurred, 
especially when models lacked sufficient context or retrieval failed. These limitations highlight 
ethical risks in using such systems for decision support, particularly in domains like healthcare, 
law, or industrial troubleshooting, where unverified AI suggestions may cause harm or 
misinformation. Future deployment should therefore integrate mechanisms such as factuality 
validation, user feedback loops, or fallback answers to mitigate risk. 
Human Evaluation and Anonymity 
This thesis included a manual human evaluation of model outputs using structured criteria: 
Factual Correctness, Relevance, Clarity, and Completeness. The evaluation was conducted by 
domain-aware individuals using a standardized Excel form. Ethical guidelines were followed 
throughout the evaluation process. Importantly, the identities of the evaluators and interview 
participants are not disclosed in this report due to privacy concerns and confidentiality 
agreements. This approach ensures protection of personal identity, in accordance with 
research ethics practices and GDPR principles. Evaluators participated voluntarily and were 
informed about the academic nature of the project. 
Societal Impact and Sustainable Development 
RAG systems have the potential to improve access to technical knowledge, increase operational 
efficiency, and reduce the burden on human support personnel. These benefits support the 
aims of UN Sustainable Development Goal 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure) 
(United Nations, n.d.). However, risks include the automation of incorrect or biased responses, 
potential deskilling of human experts, and over-reliance on machine outputs. Moreover, model

53 
 
predictions can unintentionally reflect and reinforce biases present in the training data or 
retrieved documents. 
If such systems are deployed without appropriate human oversight, they could amplify 
misinformation or affect user trust. Therefore, it is critical to balance efficiency gains with 
responsibility and accountability. Institutions must invest in AI governance frameworks that 
support fairness, transparency, and continuous monitoring. 
Final Ethical Note 
Although the primary focus of this thesis was technical evaluation, it clearly illustrates the need 
for interdisciplinary and human-centered design in AI research. As retrieval-augmented 
systems become more prominent in enterprise and public-facing applications, ethical foresight 
must remain central. Future work should explore bias detection, inclusive evaluation methods, 
and participatory design practices that incorporate diverse stakeholder perspectives. 
7 
Conclusion  
This thesis set out to evaluate and compare different retrieval strategies—sparse (BM25), dense 
(SBERT), and hybrid (Reciprocal Rank Fusion)—within a Retrieval-Augmented Generation 
(RAG) framework applied to enterprise-style document-grounded dialogue. By integrating two 
leading open-source language models, LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct, the study 
examined how different retrieval methods impact both retrieval effectiveness and the quality 
of generated responses across multiple evaluation dimensions. 
The central aim was to understand which retrieval strategies best support factual, relevant, and 
complete responses in enterprise contexts, as judged by both automated metrics (Hit@K, 
MRR, nDCG, ROUGE-L, BERTScore, Cosine Similarity) and human evaluation (Factual 
Correctness, Relevance, Clarity, Completeness). The Doc2Dial dataset served as a realistic 
testbed, and a carefully constructed evaluation pipeline ensured consistent, transparent, and 
reproducible results. 
Key findings revealed that: 
• 
Dense retrieval using SBERT consistently outperformed sparse (BM25) and hybrid 
strategies across all retrieval metrics. 
• 
In terms of generation quality, Hybrid RAG models slightly outperformed others on clarity 
and completeness, especially with Mistral-7B. 
• 
Mistral-7B generally produced more fluent and complete answers than LLaMA-2-7B, 
although both models struggled with completeness when the retriever failed to retrieve 
relevant context. 
• 
Human evaluations exposed limitations not always captured by automated metrics, 
reinforcing the need for human-centered assessment in RAG systems. 
• 
A weak or inconsistent correlation was observed between generation metrics and human 
ratings, with ROUGE-L showing the strongest alignment, particularly for relevance and 
completeness. 
While no retrieval method was universally superior, dense retrieval emerged as the most 
balanced approach, offering competitive results in both retrieval accuracy and downstream 
response quality. The study also highlighted how generator behaviour, prompt design, and 
decoding strategy all influence outcomes, underlining the need for holistic system-level tuning. 
This thesis does not offer a definitive "best" RAG configuration for all enterprise tasks but

54 
 
provides a replicable framework for comparative evaluation and identifies key trade-offs 
between retrieval strategies and language models. It contributes to the growing body of work 
emphasizing transparent evaluation, grounded generation, and ethical deployment of 
conversational AI systems in domain-specific settings. 
Looking ahead, future work could expand by including larger-scale human evaluations, 
exploring adaptive retriever-generator interaction, integrating factuality-checking modules, 
and testing on multilingual or domain-shifted datasets. Additionally, incorporating user 
feedback into evaluation loops could bridge the gap between system performance and real-
world utility. 
In addition to empirical results, this thesis contributes a structured human evaluation 
template, grounded in both practitioner interviews and established evaluation criteria. This 
template offers a transparent and replicable framework for assessing factual correctness, 
relevance, clarity, and completeness of RAG-generated responses, and can serve as a reference 
methodology for future research in this area. 
Ultimately, this research underscores the importance of retrieval design in RAG systems and 
affirms that human judgment remains essential in evaluating AI-generated content—especially 
in enterprise and decision-support environments where factual accuracy and clarity are 
critical.  
7.1 
Limitations and Future Work  
While this thesis provides valuable insights into the comparative performance of retrieval 
strategies in RAG-based conversational systems, several limitations must be acknowledged. 
These are organized according to commonly accepted dimensions of research validity to 
highlight how they may affect the interpretation and generalizability of the findings. 
Internal Validity 
Certain implementation and methodological choices may have influenced the internal 
consistency of results. The retrievers and language models used in this study were not 
specifically fine-tuned for the domain. Instead, models were evaluated using their default pre-
trained weights to maintain fairness across retrieval configurations. Although this approach 
ensures comparability, it also limits the ability to observe the potential performance 
improvements that might be achieved through domain adaptation—particularly in enterprise 
settings with specialized terminology or document structures. 
The human evaluation component, though carefully designed, was limited in scope. It 
consisted of 10 curated queries evaluated by five internal assessors. A predefined rubric and 
score averaging were used to mitigate subjectivity. However, the limited sample size restricts 
statistical robustness. Additionally, inter-rater reliability was not initially calculated due to 
privacy and design constraints, though the rationale and mitigation strategies are documented 
in Section 2.1.6.2. While prompting and citation strategies were carefully controlled, some 
generation issues such as incomplete answers or hallucinated references persisted—likely 
influenced by the models' inherent decoding behaviors. 
External Validity 
The evaluation was conducted on the validation split of the Doc2Dial dataset, which—despite 
being grounded in realistic enterprise dialogue—represents a narrow slice of potential real-
world scenarios. The dataset lacks diversity in domains, languages, and dialogue complexity,

55 
 
which constrains the generalizability of the results. Furthermore, only English-language data 
was used, and evaluation was performed by participants familiar with the experimental setup. 
Broader real-world usage may yield different outcomes, particularly in multilingual, 
multimodal, or highly specialized contexts such as healthcare, law, or e-commerce. 
Conclusion Validity 
To assess the significance of observed differences, statistical testing (ANOVA) was applied to 
the generation metrics (ROUGE-L, BERTScore F1, SBERT cosine similarity). The results 
indicated no statistically significant differences (p > 0.05) between the three retrieval 
strategies. This limits the strength of conclusions regarding the superiority of any specific 
configuration in terms of generation quality. No post-hoc pairwise comparisons were 
conducted due to the non-significant omnibus results. 
In the retrieval phase, all retrieval configurations were shared between the two language 
models (LLaMA 2 and Mistral). As a result, retrieval performance remained identical across 
models. To avoid redundancy, retrieval plots were shown only for LLaMA 2 in the Results 
section. However, this design decision was not initially clarified and may have caused 
confusion about the absence of corresponding retrieval plots for Mistral 7B, despite equivalent 
behavior across both models. 
In summary, this thesis contributes a retriever-centered evaluation of RAG systems that 
uniquely combines automated benchmarking with a structured human user study, providing 
both quantitative evidence and practitioner-grounded insights for enterprise conversational 
AI. 
7.2 
Future Work 
To address these limitations and build upon the findings, future research could explore the 
following directions: 
• 
Domain adaptation and fine-tuning: Applying retrieval and generation fine-tuning 
techniques on enterprise-specific data could yield performance gains, particularly for 
specialized terminology and document styles. 
• 
Expanded human evaluation: Increasing the number of curated questions, evaluators, 
and evaluation scenarios—potentially including real end-users—would improve statistical 
robustness and capture a broader range of qualitative judgments. 
• 
Cross-domain and multilingual benchmarking: Evaluating RAG performance on 
diverse datasets with varied domains, languages, and dialogue types would enhance 
external validity and reveal generalization patterns. 
• 
Dynamic or adaptive retrieval strategies: Investigating systems that adjust retrieval 
parameters based on question type, document structure, or conversational history may 
improve grounding and relevance. 
• 
Explainability and calibration: Incorporating confidence estimation, evidence 
scoring, or counterfactual retrieval examples could enhance user trust in RAG-generated 
responses. 
• 
Latency and deployment analysis: While this study focused on retrieval and 
generation quality, future work could also measure real-time performance trade-offs in 
latency, memory usage, and scalability, which are crucial for enterprise deployment.

Appendices  
Appendix A: Interview Materials 
A.1 Purpose of Interview 
The interviews aimed to gather expectations from customer support specialists regarding 
desirable qualities and limitations of AI-generated responses in a Retrieval-Augmented 
Generation (RAG) setup. These insights were used to design the human evaluation criteria 
for the thesis study. 
A.2 Interview Format 
• 
Type: Semi-structured. 
• 
Duration: ~30 minutes per interview. 
• 
Participants: Three domain experts from the support department (names not 
disclosed due to privacy considerations). 
• 
Medium: Online (Microsoft Teams). 
• 
Consent: Verbal consent taken; no identifying data recorded (Consent was obtained 
during Teams calls; no videos/audio stored, only anonymized text used). 
A.3 Interview Questions 
See Figure A.1 for the interview script shared with participants and a sample of collected 
responses from one interview. 
 
Figure A.1. Interview Questionnaire Used in the Study 
Appendix B: Summarized Expert Interviews 
 B.1 Interview Summary: Participant A 
Role: Object Specialist, Operations Solutions.  
Summary: 
Participant A emphasized that for AI-generated support responses to be effective, they must 
be clear, context-aware, and follow a logical sequence. Responses grounded in official 
documentation were highlighted as particularly useful for ensuring both verifiability and 
actionability. The participant stressed the importance of relevance and clarity in building

60 
 
user trust. Rather than oversimplifying complex issues, AI should provide a structured path 
to resolution, while preserving human oversight in decision-making. AI was viewed as a 
valuable assistant for quick responses, but factual correctness and groundedness were 
deemed essential in a domain-specific context. 
B.2 Interview Summary: Participant B 
Role: Object Specialist, Operations Solutions. 
Summary: 
Participant B noted that effective support responses should be accurate, well-structured, and 
professionally toned. Grounding in the internal knowledge base was seen as crucial, and the 
participant appreciated when AI-generated outputs included references for deeper 
exploration. The role of AI was framed as a supportive tool that surfaces relevant information 
but does not replace human expertise. Concerns were raised about potential over-reliance on 
AI, particularly the risks of hallucinations and biased outputs. The participant stressed that 
human judgment should always remain central to complex decision-making. 
B.3 Interview Summary: Participant C 
Role: Object Specialist, Operations Solutions. 
Summary: 
Participant C identified clarity, factual accuracy, and contextual appropriateness as key 
features of helpful AI responses. The ability to adapt and reason in follow-up conversations 
was seen as highly valuable. The participant expressed frustration with rigid or overly 
scripted systems and favored AI that responds at the user's level rather than mimicking 
corporate tones. While AI is appreciated for handling general queries, human assistance was 
preferred for sensitive or nuanced issues. Overall, AI was described as a helpful enabler when 
designed with flexibility and contextual awareness in mind. 
Appendix C: Human Evaluation Template 
The human evaluation template used in this thesis was developed to enable systematic 
assessment of RAG-generated responses. It includes structured fields for rating outputs along 
four criteria—Factual Correctness, Relevance, Clarity, and Completeness—on a 5-point Likert 
scale. The template supported side-by-side comparison of responses across different retrieval 
and generator configurations. 
Beyond its use in this study, the template represents a methodological contribution. It 
provides a transparent and replicable framework for human evaluation of RAG systems, 
which can be adapted by future research in enterprise or other domain-specific contexts.
